prompts.title: 1:1 Coaching Pack
prompts.summary: Five working prompts for the manager's most-used 1:1 patterns: pre-1:1 prep, mid-1:1 unblock, growth conversation, hard feedback, and post-1:1 followup synthesis.
prompts.why_it_matters: 1:1s compound. The difference between a routine status sync and a coaching conversation is preparation and structure. These prompts produce the artifact a manager actually uses, not generic boilerplate.
when_to_use: Before a 1:1 — to surface what to ask given the last 4 weeks of context
After a 1:1 — to extract decisions, follow-ups, and signals for your skip-level synthesis
When a 1:1 needs to shift from status to growth or from comfort to candor
When you're managing through a hard moment (PIP discussion, missed promo, project failure)
not_for: Replacing the conversation itself — these draft inputs to your own judgment, not scripts to read aloud
Logging confidential employee data into a public LLM without your company's data-handling policy
source_inspiration: Lara Hogan's 1:1 templates (BICEPS, growth questions, feedback equation).
Kim Scott's Radical Candor on care + challenge.
Michael Bungay Stanier's Coaching Habit (the seven questions).
Manager Tools 1:1 cadence and follow-up discipline.

inputs_required.name: Report context
inputs_required.description: Name (or pseudonym), level, tenure, current scope, recent project outcomes, and any open performance/career signals from prior conversations.

inputs_required.name: Manager intent
inputs_required.description: What you want this 1:1 to accomplish: status update, growth conversation, project unblock, hard feedback, or relationship repair.

inputs_required.name: Recent signals
inputs_required.description: Concrete observations from the last 2–4 weeks: peer feedback, project metrics, slack/standup tone, missed commitments, big wins.

outputs: Prep agenda the manager can scan in 5 minutes
Coaching questions tailored to the situation, not generic
Structured follow-up record that survives the next reorg
Hard-conversation script with concrete language to use and avoid

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Keep prompts grounded in evidence and avoid the LLM talking past the actual person.
prompts: Always provide concrete observations, not characterizations ('missed three commits' not 'unreliable').
Name the desired outcome of the conversation up front; don't let the LLM optimize for warmth when you need clarity.
After draft, check for advice you wouldn't actually give. Strip platitudes.

prompts.name: Pre-1:1 prep agenda
prompts.use_case: Generate a focused prep agenda for an upcoming 1:1 based on the last 4 weeks of context and your stated intent.
inputs: 3–5 concrete observations from the last 2–4 weeks (project, behavior, signal)
output_format: Section 1 — 'Open from last time': a list of unresolved follow-ups requiring revisit
Section 2 — 'Topics I want to raise': 3 items max, each with the specific observation, the question to ask, and the desired outcome
Section 3 — 'Coaching opportunities': 2 questions tailored to their growth area, in GROW format
Section 4 — 'What I will NOT bring up today': the discipline list — what to defer to keep this 1:1 focused
review_checklist: Each topic must have a SPECIFIC observation, not a characterization
Topics should not exceed 3 — more means you're using 1:1 as a status meeting, not a coaching conversation
Coaching questions should be open, not leading. ('What's making this hard?' not 'Don't you think you should…')
The 'will not bring up' list is real, not theater. Strip your own grievances if they're not material.
prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced engineering manager preparing for a 1:1 with a direct report. Generate a focused prep agenda that respects the report's time and the manager's intent.

Context
- Report: {report_context}
- Intent for this 1:1: {manager_intent}
- Recent observations (last 2–4 weeks): {observations}
- Open follow-ups from last 1:1: {open_followups}

Produce four sections:

1. Open from last time — list each unresolved follow-up with one line on its current status as I understand it.

2. Topics I want to raise — at most 3 items. Each item is structured as:
   - Observation: (specific, evidence-based, no adjectives)
   - Question: (open question I will ask first, not a statement)
   - Desired outcome: (what I want to leave the conversation knowing or having decided)

3. Coaching opportunities — exactly 2 questions in GROW format (Goal / Reality / Options / Will) tailored to their current growth edge. Avoid generic career questions.

4. What I will NOT bring up today — items I'm tempted to raise but am consciously deferring, with a one-line reason.

Keep total output under 500 words. Be specific. Avoid platitudes.

prompts.name: Mid-conversation unblock
prompts.use_case: When a 1:1 is stuck on a project blocker, generate the structured unblock conversation: surface the real obstacle, separate it from the person, name what the manager owns vs. what the report owns.
inputs: Project or initiative the report is blocked on
Their stated obstacle (in their words if possible)
What you suspect the real obstacle might be (write it down separately — don't lead with it)
output_format: 'Restate the problem' — one sentence in the report's frame, then one sentence in the manager's frame; flag the gap if they differ
'Three diagnostic questions' — open questions that test whether the stated obstacle IS the real obstacle
'Ownership map' — a 2-column list: what the report owns to unblock, what the manager owns to unblock
'Commitment ask' — the specific check-in date and what 'unblocked' looks like
review_checklist: Diagnostic questions should not embed the manager's hypothesis — they test it
Ownership map must be balanced. If the manager owns nothing, you're delegating frustration
'Unblocked' must be observable, not aspirational ('Demo working in staging by Friday' not 'making progress')

prompts.name: Growth conversation prep
prompts.use_case: Convert a status-flavored 1:1 into a growth conversation. Generate the 3-question framework that gets a report talking about their next role, not their current sprint.
inputs: Report's current level and time at level
Career direction signals you've heard (or absence of them)
Your honest read on their readiness for the next level
The growth area you most want them working on
output_format: 'Where they think they are' — opener question that surfaces their self-assessment without leading
'Where the bar is' — clear-eyed framing of what next-level looks like in this org, with 2 concrete examples of work that demonstrates it
'What to do about the gap' — coaching question that puts the agency on them, with a manager-side commitment if you have one to make
'How we'll know it's working' — an observable signal you'll both watch for over the next quarter
review_checklist: The opener does NOT presume readiness or non-readiness — let them go first
Concrete examples of next-level work should be from THIS org, not abstract ('write a cross-team RFC like Maya did for X' beats 'demonstrate strategic thinking')
Manager-side commitment is real or omitted. Don't fake one to seem supportive
Observable signal must survive the noise of a quarter — not 'speak up more' but 'present at the next eng all-hands'

prompts.name: Hard feedback delivery
prompts.use_case: Draft the language for a piece of difficult feedback you need to deliver in a 1:1. Generates the SBI-format script with the specific words to use and the words to avoid.
inputs: The specific behavior or outcome that requires feedback (Situation + Behavior — be concrete)
The Impact — on the team, the work, or the relationship — that you want to convey
Your desired outcome from the conversation: behavior change, awareness, or formal warning
output_format: 'Opening line' — the first sentence verbatim. Frames the conversation, signals it's feedback (not chitchat), invites engagement
'SBI statement' — Situation, Behavior, Impact in your own voice. Specific, observable, no characterizations
'Pause prompt' — the question you ask AFTER the SBI to invite their perspective
'Words to avoid' — 3–5 phrases the LLM noticed in the draft that water down the message or invite defensiveness
'Closing commitment' — what you want both parties to do or watch for next
review_checklist: SBI must use observable behavior, not motive ('joined the meeting 12 minutes late three times this month' not 'doesn't respect others' time')
Pause prompt should be open. Avoid 'Do you understand why this is a problem?' (closes thinking, invites compliance)
If your desired outcome is formal warning, the script must say so explicitly. Don't disguise a serious conversation as casual coaching
Read the closing commitment back to yourself. Is it something you'd be comfortable being held to as the manager?
prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced engineering manager preparing to deliver a piece of hard feedback in a 1:1. Generate a script using the SBI (Situation–Behavior–Impact) frame, with specific language and explicit pause points.

Context
- Report: {report_context}
- Situation + Behavior (be concrete and observable, not motivational): {situation_behavior}
- Impact you want to convey: {impact}
- Desired outcome: {desired_outcome} (behavior change / build awareness / formal warning)

Produce five sections:

1. Opening line — the FIRST sentence verbatim. Frames the conversation as feedback, signals it's intentional (not chitchat), invites the report into the conversation rather than putting them on trial.

2. SBI statement — written in the manager's first-person voice. Situation specific (when, where, who was there). Behavior observable, not motivational ('joined three meetings late' not 'shows disrespect for time'). Impact concrete (on the team, the work, the relationship).

3. Pause prompt — the open question you ask AFTER the SBI, before continuing. Should invite their perspective without inviting deflection. Not 'Do you understand?' but 'How does this land?' or 'What's your read on what's happening?'.

4. Words to avoid — 3 to 5 specific phrases that watered down past versions of this conversation, with a one-line reason for each. Examples: 'I just feel like maybe…', 'A lot of people are saying…', 'Don't take this the wrong way but…'.

5. Closing commitment — what both of you will do or watch for next. Concrete and time-bound. If the desired outcome is formal warning, this section must reference the formal process explicitly.

Keep total output under 500 words. Direct over warm. The kindness is in the clarity.

prompts.name: Post-1:1 follow-up synthesis
prompts.use_case: After a 1:1, turn rough notes into a structured follow-up record: decisions, action items with owners, signals to track, and what to bring back next time.
inputs: Raw notes from the 1:1 (your shorthand, transcript, or memory dump)
Any explicit commitments made by either party
output_format: Header: report name, date, duration
'Decisions made' — bullet list of any decisions, with owner
'Action items' — table with Item, Owner, Due, Definition of done
'Signals to watch' — observations to revisit at next 1:1 (engagement, scope, growth)
'For next 1:1' — the 1–2 things you want to bring back, with the prep question to revisit
'For my eyes only' — your private read on the conversation: tone, energy, anything that surprised you
review_checklist: Action items must have a SPECIFIC due date, not 'next 1:1' or 'soon'
Definition of done is observable
'Signals to watch' is signal, not interpretation. ('Hesitated when I asked about the migration' not 'seems disengaged')
The 'for my eyes only' note is for you. If it's the kind of note you wouldn't want the report to read accidentally, mark it private and store accordingly
prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced engineering manager processing rough notes from a just-finished 1:1 into a structured follow-up record.

Context
- Report name and date: {report_name_and_date}
- Raw notes (shorthand, transcript, or memory dump): {raw_notes}
- Explicit commitments made: {commitments}

Produce a clean follow-up record with these sections:

1. Header — Report name | Date | Approximate duration

2. Decisions made — bullet list. Each bullet: the decision, then the owner in parentheses. If no decisions were made, write 'None — this was a coaching/relationship 1:1' or 'None — needs follow-up'.

3. Action items — table format with columns: Item | Owner | Due | Definition of done. Due dates must be specific (a date or 'by next 1:1 on YYYY-MM-DD'), never 'soon' or 'TBD'.

4. Signals to watch — short observations to revisit at the next 1:1. These are SIGNAL not interpretation. ('Hesitated when I asked about the migration' not 'seems disengaged'.) Maximum 4 items.

5. For next 1:1 — 1 to 2 specific things to bring back, each with the prep question to revisit ('Ask about the API working group invitation — has she joined?').

6. For my eyes only — your private read: tone, energy, anything that surprised you. Mark this section as private. Two to four sentences. This is the section that compounds across many 1:1s into real understanding of the person.

Keep total output under 600 words. Faithful to the notes. Don't invent commitments.

prompts.title: Hiring Design Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for designing the hiring loop itself: role scoping, loop architecture, interviewer calibration, and candidate-facing pitch.
prompts.why_it_matters: Most hiring decisions go wrong upstream of the offer — in unclear role scopes, redundant loops, and uncalibrated interviewers. These prompts force structure before you put a candidate through the funnel.
when_to_use: Opening a new role and writing the JD
Redesigning a loop that's been hiring poorly
Onboarding new interviewers into a calibrated panel
Crafting the candidate-facing narrative
not_for: Replacing a structured intake conversation with the hiring manager and recruiter
Bypassing legal/HR review of job descriptions in regulated jurisdictions
source_inspiration: Lou Adler — Performance-Based Hiring
Google — Work Rules! on structured interviewing
Lara Hogan — Hiring blog series on calibrated panels

inputs_required.name: Role context
inputs_required.description: Level, team, scope, must-haves vs. nice-to-haves, headcount budget.

inputs_required.name: Existing loop (if any)
inputs_required.description: Stages, interviewers, signals each stage tries to capture.

inputs_required.name: What's broken
inputs_required.description: Failure modes you've observed: false positives, false negatives, slow throughput, candidate drop-off.

outputs: Loop architecture with each stage's job-to-be-done

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Force upstream thinking before recruiting starts.
prompts: State the level explicitly.
Distinguish must-haves from nice-to-haves; most loops over-weight nice-to-haves.
Each stage needs a unique job-to-be-done.

prompts.name: Role scope and JD critique
prompts.use_case: Convert a hiring-manager intake into a sharp role scope and stress-test an existing JD for vagueness, level mismatch, and inclusivity.
inputs: Top 3 outcomes the new hire must drive in their first 12 months
output_format: Section 1 — Role-in-a-paragraph: 4 sentences (work, team, scope, impact)
Section 2 — Must-haves (max 5) and nice-to-haves (max 5), with rationale tying each must-have to one of the top 3 outcomes
Section 3 — Level signals: 4–6 observable behaviors at this level, with 2 concrete examples from this org
Section 4 — JD critique: vague phrases flagged, biased language flagged, must-vs-nice imbalance flagged, technology bloat flagged
review_checklist: Each must-have maps to one of the top 3 outcomes
Level signals reference observable behaviors, not abstract competencies
JD does not list 10+ technologies (wishlist, not requirement)
Inclusive language scan: 'rockstar', 'ninja', 'culture fit', '10x' all flagged

prompts.name: Loop architecture
prompts.use_case: Design or redesign the interview loop given the role scope, with each stage having a distinct job-to-be-done.
inputs: Loop length budget (typically 4–6 stages including phone screen)
What's currently broken (false +/-, drop-off, slow throughput)
output_format: Loop table: Stage | Format | Duration | Interviewers | Job-to-be-done | Signal collected | Calibration
Stage rationale: 1 sentence per stage explaining unique purpose
Calibration plan: shadow → co-interview → calibrated, with criteria
Failure mode flags: any stage overlapping another, any signal not collected anywhere
review_checklist: Each stage has a job-to-be-done not covered by another stage
Total loop length is defensible to candidates
Phone screen has clear go/no-go, not gut feel
Calibration plan specifies what 'good' looks like
prompts.prompt:
You are designing an interview loop for a software engineering role. Given the role scope and constraints, produce a loop architecture with no redundant stages.

Context
- Role scope: {role_scope}
- Loop length budget: {loop_budget}
- What's currently broken: {whats_broken}

Produce four sections:

1. Loop table with columns: Stage | Format | Duration | Interviewers | Job-to-be-done | Signal collected | Calibration. Cover every stage from recruiter screen to closing call.

2. Stage rationale — one sentence per stage explaining unique purpose. If you can't articulate why a stage exists without overlapping another, the stage should be cut or merged.

3. Calibration plan — how a new interviewer learns the bar: progression (shadow / co-interview / lead), criteria for being calibrated, debrief checkpoint.

4. Failure mode flags — any stage overlapping another, any signal not captured anywhere, any stage with no clear go/no-go criteria.

Keep total output under 700 words. Lean toward fewer, sharper stages.

prompts.name: Interviewer calibration brief
prompts.use_case: Write the calibration brief for a new interviewer joining the loop: what to look for, what to ignore, how to write a defensible scorecard.
inputs: Stage being trained on (system design, coding, behavioral, etc.)
Role and level being interviewed for
Two examples of past hire/no-hire decisions on this stage with reasoning
output_format: 'What this stage tests' — 2–3 sentences on the signal we want
'What this stage does NOT test' — explicit list of things to ignore
'Calibration anchors' — observable behaviors mapping to strong yes / lean yes / lean no / strong no
'Common mistakes interviewers make at this stage' — 3–5 patterns to avoid
'Scorecard structure' — required sections with one line each
review_checklist: Calibration anchors reference observable candidate behavior, not interviewer feelings
'Does NOT test' list is real and specific
Common-mistakes list reflects actual mistakes the team has made
Scorecard requires evidence, not summary judgments
prompts.prompt:
You are training a new interviewer on a specific stage of the hiring loop. Write the calibration brief they'll read before their first shadow.

Context
- Stage: {stage}
- Role and level: {role_level}
- Two past decisions with reasoning: {past_decisions}

Produce five sections:

1. What this stage tests — 2–3 sentences naming the specific signal we're trying to collect.

2. What this stage does NOT test — explicit list of competencies that other stages cover.

3. Calibration anchors — for each rating (strong yes / lean yes / lean no / strong no), 2–3 observable candidate behaviors. Avoid 'demonstrated strong technical depth' — name what they did.

4. Common mistakes interviewers make at this stage — 3–5 real patterns from your team's history.

5. Scorecard structure — sections required in the written debrief, each with one line on what it must contain. Required: evidence section. Avoid 'overall impression' as a primary section.

Keep total output under 600 words. Concrete, specific, anchored in this org.

prompts.name: Candidate-facing pitch
prompts.use_case: Draft the recruiter pitch and hiring-manager pitch — talk tracks tuned to honest signals about the role and team.
inputs: Three honest things that make this role unusually attractive
Two real challenges the candidate should know up front
output_format: 'Recruiter pitch' (90 seconds) — opener, three differentiators, comp framing, next step
'Hiring manager pitch' (5 minutes) — team and mission, the work in concrete terms, growth opportunity, honest challenges, questions invitation
'Things to NOT say' — claims that won't survive due diligence
'Anticipated objections' — top 3 likely objections with honest responses
review_checklist: Differentiators specific to this role/team, not company-generic
Honest challenges are real — if you can't name two, the candidate will hear them later and feel misled
Comp framing transparent enough for self-qualification
Objection responses not defensive; if you can't answer honestly, fix the underlying issue

prompts.title: Interview Debrief Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for the post-loop debrief: scorecard validator, hire/no-hire synthesis from multiple interviewers, bias and gap audit across the loop, and offer/comp framing.
prompts.why_it_matters: The debrief is where good loops become bad decisions. Strong individual signals get washed out by groupthink, recency bias, or the loudest interviewer. These prompts force evidence and structure into the consensus.
when_to_use: Synthesizing 4–6 interviewer scorecards into a hire/no-hire recommendation
Validating a single interviewer's scorecard before it goes into the debrief
Auditing a recent set of decisions for bias or systematic blind spots
Structuring a competitive offer with honest comp framing
not_for: Replacing the live debrief conversation
Overruling the calibrated panel based on LLM-generated synthesis
source_inspiration: Google — Work Rules! on structured debriefs
First Round Review — interviews on debrief discipline
Daniel Kahneman — Thinking Fast and Slow on systematic bias correction

inputs_required.name: Interviewer scorecards
inputs_required.description: All written scorecards from the loop, with evidence sections.

inputs_required.name: Role scope
inputs_required.description: Must-haves, nice-to-haves, level signals — to anchor the debrief on the bar.

inputs_required.name: Hiring history context
inputs_required.description: Recent hires/declines on this loop, for pattern detection.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Quote the evidence; don't paraphrase to soften it.
Map every claim to a specific stage of the loop.
Flag any signal that's missing entirely from the loop.

prompts.name: Scorecard validator
prompts.use_case: Before a single interviewer's scorecard goes into the debrief, validate it against the role bar: is the evidence specific, the rating defensible, and the recommendation consistent with the evidence?
inputs: The role scope and level signals
output_format: 'Evidence quality' — strong / mixed / weak, with specific examples of vague phrasing or missing evidence
'Rating consistency' — does the rating match the evidence, or is there a gap in either direction?
'Stage fidelity' — does the scorecard assess what the stage was supposed to assess, or does it drift?
'Suggested rewrite' — 2–4 specific edits the interviewer should make before submitting
review_checklist: Evidence quality assessment quotes the actual scorecard text, not paraphrase
If rating-to-evidence has a gap, the suggested rewrite addresses it directly
Stage fidelity catches drift (e.g., a coding interviewer assessing communication style)
prompts.prompt:
You are reviewing a single interviewer's scorecard before it goes into a hiring debrief. Your job is to make the scorecard defensible, not to second-guess the interviewer's judgment.

Context
- Scorecard text: {scorecard}
- Role scope and level signals: {role_scope}
- Stage the interviewer was assigned: {stage}

Produce four sections:

1. Evidence quality — strong / mixed / weak, with specific examples of vague phrasing ('strong communicator', 'showed maturity') or missing evidence. Quote the actual text.

2. Rating consistency — does the rating (e.g., strong hire, lean no) match the evidence the interviewer cited? Flag gaps in either direction (rating too soft for the evidence, or too harsh).

3. Stage fidelity — does the scorecard assess what this stage was designed to assess, or does it drift into other stages' territory?

4. Suggested rewrite — 2 to 4 specific edits the interviewer should make. Include exact replacement phrasing where possible.

Keep total output under 500 words. Coach the interviewer; don't override them.

prompts.name: Hire / no-hire synthesis
prompts.use_case: Synthesize 4–6 interviewer scorecards into a single hire/no-hire recommendation with an evidence map across stages.
inputs: All interviewer scorecards from the loop
output_format: 'Recommendation' — strong hire / lean hire / lean no / strong no, with confidence level
'Signal map' — table: Required signal | Stage that should have caught it | Evidence collected | Gaps
'Strengths summary' — 3–5 bullets, each tied to a specific scorecard quote
'Concerns summary' — 3–5 bullets, each tied to a specific scorecard quote
'Open questions for the debrief' — what the panel still needs to discuss
'If we proceed' — what to validate in references and onboarding
review_checklist: Every claim in strengths/concerns is anchored in a scorecard quote
Signal map shows which required signals were NOT collected anywhere
Open questions are real (the panel needs to resolve them), not rhetorical
If recommendation is lean hire, the 'if we proceed' section is non-trivial
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing the full set of interviewer scorecards into a hire/no-hire recommendation for a senior engineering role. Be honest about gaps and dissent.

Context
- Scorecards: {scorecards}
- Role scope and level signals: {role_scope}
- Explicit dissent raised: {dissent}

Produce six sections:

1. Recommendation — strong hire / lean hire / lean no / strong no, with confidence level (high / medium / low). One sentence rationale.

2. Signal map — table with columns: Required signal | Stage that should have caught it | Evidence collected | Gaps. Required signals from the role scope. Flag any signal not captured anywhere.

3. Strengths summary — 3 to 5 bullets, each anchored to a specific scorecard quote (with interviewer + stage).

4. Concerns summary — 3 to 5 bullets, same anchoring requirement.

5. Open questions for the debrief — what the panel still needs to discuss to resolve. Be specific.

6. If we proceed — what to validate in references and what to design into onboarding to mitigate any concerns.

Keep total output under 800 words. Faithful to evidence; do not soften or sharpen the actual scorecards.

prompts.name: Loop bias audit
prompts.use_case: Audit a recent batch of debriefs (last 5–10 candidates) for systematic bias, redundant interviewers, or signal gaps the loop is missing.
inputs: Last 5–10 hire/no-hire decisions with brief reasoning
Demographic or background data if you have it (handled per your policy)
output_format: 'Decision pattern analysis' — what kinds of candidates the loop tends to hire and decline
'Signal gap analysis' — required signals consistently NOT showing up in scorecards
'Interviewer pattern analysis' — interviewers whose votes consistently swing positive or negative beyond the panel mean
'Recommended adjustments' — 2–4 specific changes to the loop, calibration, or panel composition
review_checklist: Pattern analysis distinguishes signal from small-sample noise (10 decisions is not statistical)
Interviewer pattern analysis is for calibration, not for blame
Adjustments are specific (e.g., 'add a written-communication signal to round 3', not 'improve calibration')
prompts.prompt:
You are auditing the last 5–10 hiring decisions on a single loop for systematic patterns: bias, signal gaps, or interviewer drift.

Context
- Recent decisions with reasoning: {recent_decisions}
- Loop architecture: {loop}
- Demographic/background data (handle per company policy): {demo_data}

Produce four sections:

1. Decision pattern analysis — what kinds of candidates the loop tends to hire vs. decline. Distinguish signal from small-sample noise (10 decisions is not statistical, but it can surface hypotheses to investigate).

2. Signal gap analysis — required signals from the role scope that consistently do NOT appear in scorecards. These are blind spots in the loop, not in the candidates.

3. Interviewer pattern analysis — interviewers whose votes consistently swing positive or negative beyond the panel mean. This is for calibration, not blame.

4. Recommended adjustments — 2 to 4 specific changes (loop stage edit, interviewer recalibration, panel composition change). Specific, not 'improve calibration'.

Keep total output under 600 words. Be careful with bias claims; surface hypotheses to investigate, do not assert.

prompts.name: Offer framing and comp story
prompts.use_case: Draft the closing call talk track and the offer letter narrative — comp, equity, growth, with honest framing of trade-offs vs. competing offers the candidate may have.
inputs: Candidate's stated motivations and other-offer context
Offer details (base, equity, signing, level, scope)
Comp band context (where this offer sits in the band)
Three things you'd genuinely commit to in the first year
output_format: 'Closing call talk track' — opener, structure of the offer in plain language, comp framing, equity story, growth commitment, candidate questions
'Offer letter narrative' — 2–3 paragraphs that go alongside the formal offer document
'Comp objection responses' — top 3 likely comp objections with honest framing
'Walk-away criteria' — what you should NOT counter to win this candidate
review_checklist: Comp framing is honest about band position
Equity story explains vesting and dilution risk in plain language
Growth commitments are real, not aspirational
Walk-away criteria are explicit before negotiation starts

prompts.title: 30/60/90 Onboarding Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for new-hire onboarding: day-1 setup checklist, weekly 1:1 prep across the 90 days, day-30/60/90 checkpoint guides, and the day-90 retrospective synthesis.
prompts.why_it_matters: The first 90 days set the trajectory. Manager's instinct is to assume the new hire will figure it out. They won't — at least not as fast or as well as they could with structure. These prompts produce the structured artifacts the manager doesn't always have time to write themselves.
when_to_use: Day-1 setup before a new hire arrives
Weekly 1:1 prep during the 90-day onboarding
Each of the 30/60/90 day checkpoints
Day-90 retrospective and forward-direction planning
not_for: Replacing the manager's actual relationship-building with the new hire
Treating onboarding as a checklist instead of a conversation
source_inspiration: Michael Watkins — The First 90 Days
Lara Hogan — onboarding playbooks
GitLab Handbook — onboarding flows

inputs_required.name: Onboarding type
inputs_required.description: New IC vs. new manager; external hire vs. internal promotion.

inputs_required.name: Role context
inputs_required.description: Level, scope, team they're joining, mission.

inputs_required.name: Day in onboarding
inputs_required.description: Day 1 setup, week 2, week 4, day 30, day 60, day 90, etc.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Pre-stage real work for week 2.
1:1 cadence shifts from listening to coaching.
Calibrate at day 60, not day 90.

prompts.name: Day-1 setup checklist generator
prompts.use_case: Generate the manager's pre-day-1 setup checklist tailored to the role.
output_format: 'Logistics checklist' — laptop, accounts, access, calendar invites
'People setup' — buddy assignment with briefing, key intros pre-scheduled
'Reading list' — team mission, current OKRs, key recent decisions, architecture docs
'First project (IC) or first listening tour (manager)' — pre-staged
'Welcome message' — for team channel, draft included
'Manager's own prep' — what the manager needs to do before day 1
review_checklist: Logistics is complete (no day-1 surprises)
Buddy has been briefed, not just assigned
First project / listening tour is real, pre-identified
Manager's own prep includes briefings on each direct report (if hire is a manager)
prompts.prompt:
You are generating the day-1 setup checklist for a new hire. The manager's most important onboarding work happens BEFORE the new hire arrives.

Context
- Onboarding type: {onboarding_type}
- Role context: {role_context}
- Available people (buddy, partners): {people}
- First project candidates (IC) or listening tour (manager): {first_work}

Produce six sections:

1. Logistics checklist — laptop ready, accounts provisioned, access granted, week 1 calendar invites sent (1:1 with manager, intros with team, intros with cross-functional partners), dev environment instructions ready (IC).

2. People setup — buddy assigned AND briefed (not just assigned), key intros pre-scheduled. For new managers: 1:1s with each direct report pre-scheduled in week 1.

3. Reading list — team mission, current OKRs, recent product launches, key architecture docs, recent leadership comms, current customer landscape. Curated, not 'read everything in the wiki'.

4. First project (IC) — small shippable project pre-identified for week 2 (typo fix or doc update for week 1; small feature for week 2–4). OR first listening tour (manager) — list of conversations to have in weeks 1–4.

5. Welcome message — draft for team channel.

6. Manager's own prep — review the role expectations, prepare honest read on each direct report (if hiring a manager), block weekly 1:1 time, identify the buddy to brief.

Keep total under 600 words. Pre-staging beats winging it.

prompts.name: Weekly 1:1 prep during onboarding
prompts.use_case: Generate the manager's prep for a 1:1 during the 90-day onboarding period — different focus by week.
inputs: Recent observations of the new hire
output_format: 'This week's intent' — what the 1:1 should accomplish given where they are
'Topics to raise' — 2–3 specific topics with the question to open
'Coaching opportunities' — 1–2 GROW questions tailored to where they are
'What to ask about' — questions about how onboarding is going, what's missing
'What NOT to bring up this week' — discipline list
review_checklist: This week's intent matches the phase (weeks 1–4: listening; weeks 5–8: contributing; weeks 9–12: independence)
Topics are 2–3, not a status update
Coaching is appropriate to where they are (not pushing for independence in week 2)
Discipline list is real (manager's anxiety often pushes them to check on too many things too early)

prompts.name: 30/60/90 checkpoint guide
prompts.use_case: Guide the manager through a specific checkpoint discussion (day 30, 60, or 90).
inputs: Manager's read on how the new hire is doing
output_format: 'Checkpoint intent' — what this checkpoint is for
'Topics from the new hire's side' — what to ask them
'Topics from your side' — feedback to deliver
'Calibration question' — are they trending toward the next phase's expectations?
'If they're off-track' — what to address now (not later)
'Forward commitment' — what's next
review_checklist: Checkpoint intent matches the phase
Feedback is honest, anchored in observations
Calibration question is honest (don't soft-pedal a day-60 concern by waiting for day 90)
If-off-track section names the specific intervention
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing for the day-{checkpoint} onboarding checkpoint with a new hire.

Context
- Checkpoint: day {checkpoint}
- Onboarding type: {onboarding_type}
- Manager's read: {managers_read}
- Specific observations (positive and concerning): {observations}

Produce six sections:

1. Checkpoint intent — what this checkpoint is for. Day 30: have they understood the system and people? Day 60: are they contributing on real work? Day 90: are they operating with calibrated independence?

2. Topics from the new hire's side — questions to ask them: what's surprised them, what's been hardest, who they don't yet know that they should, what would make the next period better.

3. Topics from your side — feedback to deliver. Specific observations, both strengths and areas to focus. Don't save up; deliver clearly now.

4. Calibration question — are they trending toward the next phase's expectations? Honest. If they're at day 30 not yet showing system fluency, name it now (not in week 8).

5. If they're off-track — specific intervention to take now: more pairing, additional ramp time, scope adjustment, manager-side change. Not 'monitor and revisit'.

6. Forward commitment — what's next: scope, growth direction, next checkpoint date.

Keep total under 600 words. Honest checkpoint; calibrate now, not later.

prompts.name: Day-90 retrospective synthesis
prompts.use_case: Synthesize the day-90 onboarding retrospective into a forward direction plan.
inputs: Manager's comprehensive read on the new hire
Forward scope and growth direction agreed
output_format: 'Onboarding retrospective' — what worked, what didn't, what to change for next hire
'Where they are at day 90' — calibration to the level (operating at level / approaching / below)
'Strengths confirmed' — 3 strengths with specific evidence
'Growth areas for next 90 days' — 2 to 3, specific
'Scope and direction' — what they own going forward, what's next
'Manager commitments' — coaching, sponsorship, intros for the next 90 days
review_checklist: Onboarding retrospective produces actionable changes for next hire
Calibration is honest; don't soft-pedal 'below'
Strengths and growth areas anchored in specific evidence
Manager commitments are specific
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing the day-90 onboarding retrospective into a forward direction plan.

Context
- Notes from day-90 conversation: {day_90_notes}
- Manager's comprehensive read: {managers_read}
- Onboarding feedback they shared: {onboarding_feedback}
- Forward scope and growth direction agreed: {forward_direction}

Produce six sections:

1. Onboarding retrospective — what worked in the onboarding, what didn't, what to change for the next hire. Convert their feedback to specific changes for next time.

2. Where they are at day 90 — calibration to the level: operating at level / approaching / below. Honest. If 'below', the conversation needs the underperformance-diagnosis worksheet next.

3. Strengths confirmed — 3 strengths with specific evidence (named projects, specific quotes from peer feedback).

4. Growth areas for next 90 days — 2 to 3, specific, anchored in observation. NOT 'communication' but 'leading the technical-design discussion in cross-team meetings without me'.

5. Scope and direction — what they own going forward, what's next, how their role evolves over the next 90 days.

6. Manager commitments — coaching cadence, sponsorship for specific opportunities, intros for the next 90 days. Real commitments.

Keep total under 700 words. Forward-looking, calibrated, specific.

prompts.estimated_time: 30–60 minutes per prompt; full onboarding spans 90 days

prompts.title: Performance Review Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for the performance review cycle: drafting from raw inputs, calibration prep brief, delivery script, and self-assessment critique.
prompts.why_it_matters: Performance reviews are weaponized when they're vague and forgotten when they're soft. These prompts force evidence, calibration discipline, and a delivery that lands.
when_to_use: Drafting a performance review from 1:1 notes, project artifacts, and peer feedback
Preparing for calibration discussions with peer managers
Practicing the delivery script for a hard review
Critiquing an employee's self-assessment for gaps and over/under-claims
not_for: Generating a review from generic competencies with no specific evidence
Replacing your manager's calibration judgment
source_inspiration: Patty McCord — Powerful, on radical clarity
Kim Scott — Radical Candor, on care + challenge in delivery
Lara Hogan — Resilient Management, on calibration mechanics

inputs_required.name: Employee context
inputs_required.description: Level, time at level, scope, last review's narrative.

inputs_required.name: Evidence corpus
inputs_required.description: 1:1 notes from the cycle, peer feedback, project artifacts, metrics.

inputs_required.name: Career ladder text
inputs_required.description: The leveling rubric this employee is being assessed against.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Quote evidence; don't paraphrase to soften.
Map every assessment to a ladder behavior.
If your draft is comfortable to read, it's probably too soft.

prompts.name: Performance review draft
prompts.use_case: Draft the written review from raw inputs (1:1 notes, peer feedback, project artifacts), structured against the career ladder.
inputs: Evidence corpus (1:1 notes, peer feedback, projects, metrics)
Career ladder text for current level
output_format: 'Headline' — 1 sentence summarizing the cycle
'Strengths' — 3–5 bullets, each anchored in a specific event/quote with date
'Areas for growth' — 2–4 bullets, each anchored in evidence and tied to a ladder behavior they're not yet meeting
'Specific examples' — 4–6 narrative paragraphs, each one event with what was demonstrated and what could have been better
'Overall rating recommendation' — with rationale and confidence
'Things I considered but did not include' — context that might have shifted the rating but didn't
review_checklist: Every strength/growth bullet has at least one evidence anchor
Growth bullets reference ladder behaviors, not vague 'soft skills'
Specific examples are events, not characterizations
Headline matches the rating recommendation
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting a performance review for an engineer based on the cycle's evidence. The review must be evidence-anchored, calibrated to the career ladder, and deliverable in person.

Context
- Employee: {employee_context}
- Evidence corpus: {evidence}
- Career ladder for current level: {ladder}
- Time period: {period}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — 1 sentence summarizing the cycle.

2. Strengths — 3 to 5 bullets, each anchored in a specific event or quote with date. No 'strong communicator' without a specific instance.

3. Areas for growth — 2 to 4 bullets, each anchored in evidence and tied to a specific ladder behavior they're not yet meeting consistently.

4. Specific examples — 4 to 6 narrative paragraphs, each describing one event: what was demonstrated, what could have been better. Use names, dates, projects.

5. Overall rating recommendation — with rationale and confidence level. Should match the headline.

6. Things I considered but did not include — context that might have shifted the rating but didn't. This is for the manager's calibration prep, not the employee's copy.

Keep total output under 1000 words. If your draft is comfortable to read, it's probably too soft.

prompts.name: Calibration prep brief
prompts.use_case: Prepare a 1-page brief for the calibration meeting where peer managers will challenge or accept your rating recommendation.
inputs: Names of peer engineers at this level for comparison
Calibration meeting format and time per case
output_format: 'Rating recommendation and one-line rationale'
'Top 3 evidence pieces' — the strongest cases for this rating
'Anticipated challenges' — what peer managers are likely to push back on, with response
'Comparison anchors' — how this employee compares to 2–3 peers at the level (without naming them in the manager's copy)
'My confidence level and what would change my mind'
review_checklist: Top 3 evidence pieces are events, not impressions
Anticipated challenges are real (you've had calibration before, you know what gets pushed)
Comparison anchors are honest, not advocacy
Confidence level is honest; if you're at 'low', say so

prompts.name: Review delivery script
prompts.use_case: Draft the delivery script for the in-person review conversation: opener, the hard parts, pause prompts, the ask.
inputs: What the employee likely expects (their self-assessment, recent 1:1 sentiment)
What you most want them to take away
output_format: 'Opener' — first 30 seconds: frame the conversation, signal the structure
'Strengths delivery' — quick, specific, then move on
'The hard part' — verbatim language for the most difficult feedback
'Pause prompts' — questions to invite their reaction
'Forward commitment' — what you'll both do next
'Words to avoid' — phrases that will undercut the message
review_checklist: Opener does not bury the rating in chit-chat
Hard part uses observable language, not characterization
Pause prompts are open ('How does this land?' not 'Do you understand?')
Words to avoid include real watering-down phrases ('I just feel like…')
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing the delivery script for a performance review conversation. The structure should land the message clearly, with care and without softening.

Context
- Review draft: {review}
- Final rating: {rating}
- What the employee likely expects: {expectations}
- What you most want them to take away: {takeaway}

Produce six sections:

1. Opener — the first 30 seconds. Frames the conversation, signals the structure ('We'll cover the strengths, then growth, then the rating'), and does not bury the rating in chit-chat.

2. Strengths delivery — short, specific, anchored. Move on; don't dwell to soften the harder parts.

3. The hard part — verbatim language for the most difficult feedback. Observable, not characterizing. SBI format (Situation, Behavior, Impact).

4. Pause prompts — open questions to invite their reaction. Avoid 'Do you understand why?' Use 'How does this land?' or 'What's your read?'.

5. Forward commitment — what you'll both do next. Specific and time-bound.

6. Words to avoid — 3 to 5 specific phrases the manager should not say (real watering-down patterns: 'I just feel like…', 'A lot of people are saying…', 'Don't take this the wrong way but…').

Keep total output under 600 words. Direct over warm.

prompts.name: Self-assessment critique
prompts.use_case: Critique an employee's self-assessment before the calibration meeting: gaps, over-claims, under-claims, and what to ask them about.
inputs: Manager's evidence corpus for the cycle
output_format: 'Alignment' — where the self-assessment matches manager evidence
'Over-claims' — accomplishments stated more strongly than evidence supports
'Under-claims' — accomplishments understated or missing
'Ladder behaviors discussed and missing' — which ladder dimensions they did and did not address
'Questions to ask in the review' — 3–5 specific questions the manager should ask in person
review_checklist: Over-claims are anchored to specific stated accomplishment vs. specific evidence
Under-claims include evidence the manager has that the employee did not surface
Questions to ask are open and lead to evidence, not yes/no
prompts.prompt:
You are critiquing an employee's self-assessment before a performance review. The goal is to make the in-person conversation more useful, not to grade the self-assessment.

Context
- Self-assessment text: {self_assessment}
- Manager evidence corpus: {evidence}
- Career ladder for current level: {ladder}

Produce five sections:

1. Alignment — points where the self-assessment matches the manager's evidence. Briefly.

2. Over-claims — specific stated accomplishments where the evidence is weaker than the claim. Quote both the self-assessment line and the relevant evidence.

3. Under-claims — accomplishments the manager observed that the employee did not surface. This is often where the highest-signal coaching happens.

4. Ladder behaviors discussed and missing — which ladder dimensions did the self-assessment cover, and which were skipped?

5. Questions to ask in the review — 3 to 5 specific, open questions the manager should ask in person to surface the employee's reasoning on the over- and under-claims.

Keep total output under 500 words. Goal: make the in-person conversation richer, not to mark up the self-assessment.

prompts.title: PIP and Underperformance Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for the underperformance and PIP cycle: pre-PIP diagnosis facilitation, PIP letter drafter, weekly review writer, and the PIP delivery / hard-conversation script.
prompts.why_it_matters: Most failed PIPs failed at diagnosis or delivery. These prompts force the diagnostic discipline before issuing the PIP and produce delivery language that lands as clarity rather than performance theater.
when_to_use: Working through the pre-PIP diagnosis worksheet with structured prompting
Drafting the PIP letter once diagnosis is complete and HR-aligned
Writing the weekly review summary during an active PIP
Preparing the script for the PIP delivery conversation
not_for: Replacing the manager's judgment or HR partnership
Bypassing the diagnostic discipline (don't use the PIP letter prompt without doing the diagnosis first)
source_inspiration: See pip-framework-and-letter and underperformance-diagnosis-worksheet templates
Patty McCord — Powerful, on radical clarity
Lara Hogan — Resilient Management on documented support

inputs_required.name: Specific observed events
inputs_required.description: Dated, observable instances over 8–12 weeks.

inputs_required.name: Prior feedback documentation
inputs_required.description: 1:1 notes, written feedback already given.

inputs_required.name: HR partner alignment status
inputs_required.description: Has HR been consulted? At what stage?

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Diagnosis before action; HR partnered throughout.
prompts: Don't draft the PIP letter without doing the diagnosis first.
HR is partnered, not informed.
Specific evidence; no characterizations.

prompts.name: Pre-PIP diagnosis facilitator
prompts.use_case: Guide the manager through the underperformance diagnosis worksheet, surfacing alternatives to PIP that may be the right intervention.
inputs: Observed events over last 8–12 weeks (dated, specific)
output_format: 'Evidence quality check' — is there enough specific evidence (3+ events) to diagnose, or do we need more?
'Alternative-explanation pass' — for each alternative (clarity, role fit, manager fit, team conditions, personal, comp/level, capability), what evidence supports/contradicts
'Most likely diagnosis' — with confidence level and what would change it
'Recommended intervention' — one of seven options (coaching, role change, manager intervention, structural, PIP, managed exit, continue monitoring)
'Next step this week' — specific action
review_checklist: Insufficient-evidence flag triggers when fewer than 3 specific events
Alternative-explanation pass is rigorous; no skipping uncomfortable alternatives
Confidence level is honest
Recommended intervention is one of the seven, not stacked
prompts.prompt:
You are facilitating an underperformance diagnosis with an engineering manager. The discipline: rule out alternatives before recommending PIP.

Context
- Observed events (last 8–12 weeks): {events}
- Prior feedback given (with dates): {prior_feedback}
- Manager's current working hypothesis: {hypothesis}

Produce five sections:

1. Evidence quality check — is there enough specific evidence (3+ events) to diagnose, or does the manager need to gather more over the next 4–6 weeks? If fewer than 3 specific events, the answer is 'gather more, do not act now'.

2. Alternative-explanation pass — for EACH alternative below, what evidence supports it, what contradicts it: clarity (have we set clear expectations?), role fit (is the work right for them?), manager fit (have we managed them well?), team conditions (is the team functional?), personal circumstance, comp/level mismatch, capability gap.

3. Most likely diagnosis — best candidate with confidence level (high / medium / low). What evidence would change the diagnosis.

4. Recommended intervention — exactly ONE of these seven, not stacked: coaching + clarity (clarity issue), role change (role fit), manager intervention (manager fit), structural fix (team conditions), PIP (capability + sustained pattern), managed exit (decision is already made), continue monitoring (insufficient evidence).

5. Next step this week — specific action: 'Document expectations in writing by Friday' / 'Talk to HR partner Monday' / 'Gather 4 more weeks of evidence before deciding'.

Keep total under 700 words. Diagnosis discipline; not advocacy for PIP.

prompts.name: PIP letter drafter
prompts.use_case: After diagnosis and HR alignment, draft the co-signed PIP letter with observable success criteria and explicit manager support.
inputs: Performance gap with specific examples and dates
Career-ladder behaviors at current level the gap touches
output_format: 'Statement of purpose' — neutral, factual
'Performance gap table' — Expected at level | Observed in last N months
'Specific examples' — events with dates
'Success criteria table' — Criterion | Measurement | Cadence of review (each criterion observable and measurable)
'Manager support commitments' — specific (not 'increased coaching')
'What is not changing' — comp, benefits, scope, reporting
'Acknowledgment' — receipt and understanding signature lines
review_checklist: Each success criterion is observable and measurable (not 'improve communication')
Examples are specific events, not characterizations
Manager support is real, specific commitments
Letter is defensible to HR/legal review

prompts.name: PIP weekly review summary
prompts.use_case: After each weekly PIP review meeting, produce the written summary that goes to the report and HR partner within 24 hours.
inputs: Status per success criterion (on track / off track / unclear)
output_format: 'Header' — employee name, week N of total, date
'Criteria status this week' — per criterion, status with one-line evidence
'Manager observations' — what the manager saw beyond the criteria check
'Commitments for next week' — employee and manager commitments
'Next review date'
review_checklist: Each criterion status has specific evidence
Manager observations are observable, not characterizing
Commitments are specific and time-bound
Tone is professional and clear, not warm or harsh

prompts.name: PIP delivery conversation script
prompts.use_case: Draft the script for the in-person PIP delivery conversation. The kindness is in the clarity.
inputs: Employee context (likely reaction, prior feedback level)
Whether HR partner will be present
output_format: 'Pre-conversation prep' — manager's own preparation
'Opening (verbatim)' — first 60 seconds
'Walking through the letter' — how to cover each section
'Pause prompts' — open questions inviting their reaction
'Common reactions and responses' — anger, denial, tears, negotiation, withdrawal — with how to handle each
'Closing' — the commitment, the next-step logistics, the door-open
review_checklist: Opening does not bury the lede; PIP is named within 60 seconds
Walking-through is structured, not improvised
Pause prompts are open questions, not yes/no
Reactions section addresses real likely reactions, not generic ones
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing the manager to deliver a PIP in person. Hardest conversation in management; structure helps.

Context
- PIP letter (final): {letter}
- Employee context (likely reaction, prior feedback level): {employee_context}
- HR partner present: {hr_present}
- Setting: {setting}

Produce six sections:

1. Pre-conversation prep — manager's own preparation: review letter aloud once, brief HR partner, confirm room/setting, do your own emotional processing in advance.

2. Opening (verbatim) — first 60 seconds. PIP is named within 60 seconds. Example: 'I'm sharing a Performance Improvement Plan with you today. This is a formal process to address specific gaps in your performance against the expectations for your level. I want to walk you through it together.'

3. Walking through the letter — how to cover each section: statement of purpose, performance gap, examples, success criteria, manager support, what's not changing, acknowledgment.

4. Pause prompts — open questions inviting their reaction at each major section: 'How does this land?' / 'What questions do you have about this?' / 'Where are you not aligned with this assessment?'

5. Common reactions and responses — for each likely reaction, how to handle: anger (don't escalate, listen, restate the facts), denial (don't argue, acknowledge they see it differently, restate the criteria), tears (offer pause, water, time), negotiation (don't negotiate the PIP itself; can negotiate specific support), withdrawal (give time, schedule follow-up).

6. Closing — the commitment to the weekly cadence, the next-step logistics, an open door for follow-up questions.

Keep total under 700 words. Direct, warm, specific.

prompts.estimated_time: 60–120 minutes per prompt; PIP cycle runs 30–90 days

prompts.title: Promotion and Growth Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for promotion and growth: case construction, devil's-advocate stress test, growth plan from current to next level, and the 'not yet' delivery script.
prompts.why_it_matters: Promotion cases that succeed in calibration are evidence-rich and stress-tested. Cases that fail are aspirational. Growth conversations either accelerate readiness or papered over the gap. These prompts handle both ends of the cycle.
when_to_use: Constructing a promotion case for calibration
Stress-testing your own case before the calibration meeting
Drafting a 6–12 month growth plan to close the gap to the next level
Delivering 'not yet' to a high-performer who expected promotion this cycle
not_for: Manufacturing a case where the evidence does not support the level
Replacing your judgment about readiness — these structure the argument, not make it
source_inspiration: Will Larson — Staff Engineer, on level archetypes
Camille Fournier — The Manager's Path, on growth conversations
Lara Hogan — Resilient Management, on promotion case mechanics

inputs_required.name: Employee context
inputs_required.description: Current level, time at level, scope, recent reviews.

inputs_required.name: Career ladder
inputs_required.description: Both current and target level rubrics.

inputs_required.name: Evidence corpus
inputs_required.description: Projects, peer feedback, scope changes, written artifacts.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Cite ladder behaviors at the target level (not current).
Distinguish demonstrated behaviors from one-off events.
Honest evidence beats persuasive narrative.

prompts.name: Promotion case construction
prompts.use_case: Build the written promotion case structured against the target-level career ladder, with evidence anchored to specific events.
inputs: Employee context (current level, time at level, scope)
Career ladder for both current and target level
Evidence corpus (projects, peer feedback, written artifacts)
What they've explicitly been working on this cycle
output_format: 'Headline' — 2 sentences: who, current level, recommended target level, time at level
'Operating at target level' — for each ladder dimension at the target level, evidence with dates
'Scope demonstrated' — projects/initiatives that show target-level scope, with outcomes
'Peer signal' — quotes from peer feedback or cross-team partners
'Risks and mitigations' — gaps that calibration will likely raise, with how the employee will close them
'My confidence' — high / medium / low and what would change my mind
review_checklist: Every ladder behavior cites a specific event with date
Scope demonstrated is at TARGET level, not stretch examples at current level
Risks and mitigations are real (don't paper over)
Confidence level is honest
prompts.prompt:
You are constructing a promotion case for an engineer at the {current_level} level being recommended for promotion to {target_level}. The case will be evaluated in calibration against peers at the target level.

Context
- Employee: {employee_context}
- Ladder for both levels: {ladder}
- Evidence corpus: {evidence}
- What they've been working on this cycle: {focus_areas}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences: who, current level, recommended target level, time at level.

2. Operating at target level — for each ladder dimension at the target level, the evidence that they're already operating there, with dates and specific events. NOT 'they've shown growth toward X' but 'they're already doing X, see Y'.

3. Scope demonstrated — projects or initiatives that show target-level scope, with outcomes. Specific.

4. Peer signal — quotes from peer feedback or cross-team partners. Direct quotes preferred.

5. Risks and mitigations — gaps calibration will likely raise. For each, how the employee is closing it (not 'will close').

6. My confidence (high / medium / low) and what would change my mind.

Keep total output under 1200 words. Anchored in evidence at TARGET level, not stretch performance at current.

prompts.name: Promotion case devil's advocate
prompts.use_case: Stress-test your own promotion case before calibration: where is the evidence weak, where is it stretch performance vs. consistent, where will peer managers push back?
inputs: Recent calibration patterns (what gets pushed back on this team)
output_format: 'Strongest 3 evidence points' — what the case rests on
'Weakest 3 evidence points' — where the case is most vulnerable
'One-off events vs. consistent demonstration' — for each ladder behavior cited, is the evidence one event or a pattern?
'Likely push-back from calibration' — anticipated specific questions
'Recommended edits' — concrete changes to strengthen the case before submission
'Honest verdict' — would you advance this case yourself in calibration if the employee weren't yours?
review_checklist: Devil's advocate is genuinely adversarial, not gentle
One-off vs consistent distinction is honest
Recommended edits are concrete (add evidence X, remove claim Y)
Honest verdict is the gut check; don't game it
prompts.prompt:
You are playing devil's advocate against a promotion case before it goes into calibration. Your job is to find what's weak so the manager can fix it (or pull the case) before the room does it for them.

Context
- Promotion case draft: {case_draft}
- Career ladder for target level: {target_ladder}
- Recent calibration patterns: {calibration_patterns}

Produce six sections:

1. Strongest 3 evidence points — what the case rests on. Quote them.

2. Weakest 3 evidence points — where the case is most vulnerable. Quote them.

3. One-off events vs. consistent demonstration — for each ladder behavior cited, is the evidence ONE event or a PATTERN? Promotion is for consistent target-level operation, not single instances of stretch.

4. Likely push-back from calibration — anticipated specific questions or challenges from peer managers. Specific, not generic.

5. Recommended edits — concrete changes to strengthen the case. 'Add evidence of cross-team partnership in Q2' beats 'add more evidence'.

6. Honest verdict — if this employee weren't yours, would you advance this case in calibration? Yes / No / Not yet, with reason. This is the gut check; don't game it.

Keep total output under 700 words. Adversarial in service of the manager and employee.

prompts.name: Growth plan to next level
prompts.use_case: Build a 6–12 month growth plan from current level to target level: what behaviors to demonstrate, what scope to take on, what coaching support, what milestones.
inputs: Career ladder gap analysis (what behaviors at target are not yet demonstrated)
Available scope/projects in the next 12 months
output_format: 'Gap summary' — 3–5 specific behaviors at target level not yet demonstrated
'Scope opportunities' — projects or initiatives in the next 12 months that would naturally surface those behaviors
'Behavioral commitments' — what the employee commits to doing differently
'Manager support' — what the manager commits to (coaching, sponsorship, intros)
'Quarterly milestones' — what 'on track' looks like at +3, +6, +9 months
'Honest reality' — is this 6 months out, 12, 18+? Don't promise faster than reality
review_checklist: Gap summary references specific ladder language
Scope opportunities are real (not 'find a stretch project')
Behavioral commitments are observable
Manager support is specific commitments, not platitudes
Honest reality is honest — don't sell hope
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting a 6–12 month growth plan for an engineer working toward the next level. This is a working document the employee and manager will revisit each quarter.

Context
- Employee: {employee_context}
- Career ladder gap (target behaviors not yet demonstrated): {gap}
- Available scope/projects in next 12 months: {scope_opportunities}
- Manager's coaching capacity: {coaching_capacity}

Produce six sections:

1. Gap summary — 3 to 5 specific behaviors at target level not yet demonstrated. Reference specific ladder language.

2. Scope opportunities — projects or initiatives in the next 12 months that would naturally surface those behaviors. Real projects, not 'a stretch project'.

3. Behavioral commitments — what the employee commits to doing differently. Observable. ('Lead the cross-team kickoff for project X by Q3' beats 'demonstrate strategic thinking'.)

4. Manager support — what the manager commits to (coaching cadence, sponsorship for specific opportunities, intros to specific people). Real commitments.

5. Quarterly milestones — what 'on track' looks like at +3, +6, +9 months. Observable.

6. Honest reality — is this 6 months out, 12, or 18+? Don't sell hope you can't back. The kindness is in the honest timeline.

Keep total output under 800 words. Working doc; clarity over polish.

prompts.name: 'Not yet' delivery script
prompts.use_case: Draft the delivery script for telling a high-performer they're not getting promotion this cycle, with the specific gap and a real growth plan.
inputs: What they likely expect (recent signals, 1:1 conversations)
The specific gap that drove the not-yet decision
The growth plan you're committing to (or have already drafted)
output_format: 'Opener' — first 30 seconds, doesn't bury the lede
'The decision and the why' — verbatim language naming the gap
'What this is NOT' — what the not-yet does not mean (e.g., not a performance issue, not loss of confidence)
'Pause prompt' — invite their reaction
'The forward path' — the growth plan and timeline, with manager commitments
'What you should not say' — phrases that will undermine trust
review_checklist: Opener says it's a not-yet within the first 60 seconds; no slow-walking
The why uses observable language, not 'the calibration room felt'
What this is NOT is real (don't claim 'no performance issue' if there is one)
Forward path includes a real timeline, not 'next cycle' if you can't commit
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing to tell a high-performer they're not getting promoted this cycle. The kindness is in the clarity of the gap and the realness of the forward path.

Context
- Employee: {employee_context}
- What they likely expect: {expectations}
- The specific gap that drove the not-yet: {gap}
- The growth plan you're committing to: {growth_plan}

Produce six sections:

1. Opener — the first 30 seconds. Does NOT bury the lede in chit-chat or strengths. Within 60 seconds the employee should know it's a not-yet.

2. The decision and the why — verbatim language naming the gap. Observable, evidence-anchored. NOT 'the calibration room felt' but 'the case did not yet show consistent X'.

3. What this is NOT — what the not-yet does not mean. Be honest: don't claim 'no performance issue' if there is one. Common: 'this is not a loss of confidence in your trajectory'.

4. Pause prompt — open question inviting their reaction. 'How is this landing?' / 'What's your read?'

5. The forward path — the growth plan and timeline, with specific manager commitments (sponsorship, scope, coaching cadence). Real commitments.

6. What you should not say — 3 to 5 phrases that will undermine trust. ('It was so close', 'next time for sure', 'the room just didn't get it'.)

Keep total output under 600 words. Direct, warm, specific.

prompts.title: Build vs. Buy Decision Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for build-vs-buy analysis: TCO calculator (build side), TCO calculator (buy side), strategic-vs-commodity assessment, and reversibility / one-way-door analysis.
prompts.why_it_matters: Build-vs-buy decisions go wrong when build cost is under-estimated and buy cost is over-estimated. These prompts force comparable analysis: full cost on both sides, the commodity-vs-strategic check that determines which way the decision should bend, and the reversibility analysis that prevents zombie commitments.
when_to_use: Calculating honest TCO for the build option
Calculating honest TCO for the buy option
Testing whether the capability is genuinely strategic or just feels strategic
Assessing reversibility before committing
not_for: Replacing the build-vs-buy memo template — these support the analysis, not replace it
Bypassing finance/CFO partner for cost validation
source_inspiration: FinOps Foundation — TCO patterns
Will Larson — Staff Engineer on platform investment
Standard EA build-vs-buy frameworks

inputs_required.name: Capability description
inputs_required.description: What we're deciding about, who consumes it.

inputs_required.name: Build option scope
inputs_required.description: What we'd build, MVP and target state.

inputs_required.name: Buy option vendors
inputs_required.description: 1–3 vendor candidates if buy is on the table.

inputs_required.name: Strategic context
inputs_required.description: Where this fits in the company's strategy.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Honest TCO; check the strategic claim.
prompts: Build cost is mostly operating cost, not initial dev.
Buy TCO includes implementation, ongoing ops, exit cost.
If the capability is strategic, the case should be stronger than 'we want to build it'.

prompts.name: Build TCO calculator
prompts.use_case: Calculate the full 3-year TCO for the build option, including the operating cost that's typically the dominant cost.
inputs: Engineering team size and fully-loaded cost
Estimated ongoing maintenance / evolution / support cost as % of initial dev annually
output_format: 'Initial development cost' — engineer-weeks × fully-loaded weekly cost
'Year 1 operating cost' — maintenance, support, evolution
'Years 2–3 operating cost' — typically grows as scope/users grow
'Infrastructure cost' — cloud, tooling, third-party services the build depends on
'3-year TCO' — total
'Sensitivity' — what TCO becomes if dev takes 1.5x estimate, or operating cost is 50% of dev annually instead of 30%
review_checklist: Operating cost is realistic (typically 30–70% of initial dev cost annually)
Fully-loaded engineering cost includes benefits, tooling, overhead (typically 1.4–1.6x salary)
Sensitivity analysis is honest about estimation bias (engineering estimates skew low)
Infrastructure dependencies are included (the build doesn't run on air)
prompts.prompt:
You are calculating honest 3-year TCO for the BUILD option. Build cost is mostly operating, not initial dev.

Context
- Build scope (MVP and target state): {build_scope}
- Engineering team size and fully-loaded weekly cost: {team_cost}
- Estimated initial development time: {dev_time}
- Operating cost as % of initial dev annually: {ops_pct}

Produce six sections:

1. Initial development cost — engineer-weeks × fully-loaded weekly cost. Show calculation. Include design, dev, testing, integration, launch.

2. Year 1 operating cost — maintenance, support, evolution. Typically 30–70% of initial dev cost annually. Show calculation.

3. Years 2–3 operating cost — typically grows 10–20% per year as scope and users grow.

4. Infrastructure cost — cloud, tooling, third-party services the build depends on. Don't forget these.

5. 3-year TCO — total all-in.

6. Sensitivity — TCO if dev takes 1.5x estimate (engineering estimation bias is documented and large), or if operating cost is 50% of initial dev annually instead of 30%. Both are common.

Keep total under 600 words. Engineering estimates skew low; build sensitivity into the model.

prompts.name: Buy TCO calculator
prompts.use_case: Calculate the full 3-year TCO for the buy option, including implementation, ongoing operations, and exit cost.
inputs: Ongoing operations cost (admin, training, monitoring of the vendor)
Likely exit / switching cost in year 3
output_format: 'Year 1 cost' — license + implementation
'Year 2–3 cost' — license + ongoing operations
'Hidden costs' — engineer time on integration and ongoing operations
'Opportunity cost' — what the team's not doing because they're operating this vendor
'Exit cost' — what it would cost to switch in year 3 if needed
'3-year TCO' — total
'TCO scenarios' — base, with 50% usage growth, with vendor price increase 15% in year 2
review_checklist: Implementation cost is realistic, not vendor-quoted
Hidden costs (engineer time) are quantified
Exit cost is included (switching cost is part of TCO of choosing the vendor)
Scenarios include realistic adverse movements
prompts.prompt:
You are calculating honest 3-year TCO for the BUY option. Sticker price is the smallest part.

Context
- Vendor pricing: {vendor_pricing}
- Implementation cost (eng time + services + integration): {implementation}
- Ongoing ops cost (admin, training, monitoring): {ongoing_ops}
- Likely exit / switching cost in year 3: {exit_cost}

Produce seven sections:

1. Year 1 cost — license + implementation. Implementation cost should be HONEST, not vendor-quoted (vendors systematically under-quote implementation).

2. Year 2–3 cost — license + ongoing operations. Account for usage-based growth.

3. Hidden costs — engineer time on integration (often 0.5–2 engineer-quarters), ongoing operations (a fraction of an engineer's time forever), training cost, observability of the vendor.

4. Opportunity cost — what your team is NOT doing because they're operating this vendor. Real cost even if not in the budget.

5. Exit cost — what it would cost to switch in year 3 (replatform cost, dual-running cost, data migration). This is part of the TCO of choosing this vendor.

6. 3-year TCO — total all-in.

7. TCO scenarios — base case, with 50% usage growth (cost inflation if usage-based), with vendor price increase 15% in year 2 (common at renewal).

Keep total under 700 words. Sticker price is theater; TCO is the real cost.

prompts.name: Strategic-vs-commodity assessment
prompts.use_case: Test whether the capability is genuinely strategic (build) or commodity (buy). Most teams overclaim strategic.
inputs: What competitors do for this capability
output_format: 'Strategic claim test' — is this capability part of our differentiated value to customers?
'Commodity check' — what % of comparable companies just buy this?
'Customer visibility' — do customers see / experience this capability directly?
'Optionality value' — does building preserve options that buying forecloses?
'Honest assessment' — strategic / supporting / commodity
'Recommendation bias' — strategic → bias to build; commodity → bias to buy; supporting → analyze on TCO and reversibility
review_checklist: Strategic claim test challenges the manager's instinct (most overclaim)
Commodity check uses real industry data, not vibes
Honest assessment is hard; if 80% of peers buy, this is probably commodity
Recommendation bias is just bias; the final decision still uses TCO and reversibility
prompts.prompt:
You are testing whether a capability is strategic (build it) or commodity (buy it). Most teams overclaim strategic; this prompt is the honesty check.

Context
- Capability description: {capability}
- Company strategy and positioning: {strategy}
- What competitors do: {competitor_practice}
- Customer-facing visibility: {customer_visibility}

Produce six sections:

1. Strategic claim test — is this capability part of our differentiated value to customers? Does our customer choose us partly because of this? Is it on our marketing site as a differentiator?

2. Commodity check — what percentage of comparable companies just buy this from a vendor? If 80%+ buy, this is commodity territory regardless of how it feels internally.

3. Customer visibility — do customers see and experience this capability directly, or is it internal infrastructure they never know about? Customer-facing → more strategic case for build. Internal infrastructure → likely commodity.

4. Optionality value — does building preserve options that buying forecloses? Examples: building gives you data ownership (can analyze customer behavior); buying might mean exposing data to a vendor.

5. Honest assessment — strategic / supporting / commodity. Be willing to land on commodity even if it's uncomfortable.

6. Recommendation bias — strategic capabilities bias toward build (build the differentiator); commodity capabilities bias toward buy (don't reinvent table-stakes); supporting capabilities are analyzed on TCO and reversibility. The bias is just an input; the final decision uses the TCO and reversibility analysis.

Keep total under 600 words. Honesty over narrative.

prompts.name: Reversibility analysis
prompts.use_case: Analyze the reversibility of build vs. buy choices. One-way doors warrant more caution than two-way doors.
inputs: Buy option (with vendor and contract terms)
Time horizon for considering reversibility (typically 18–36 months)
output_format: 'Build → switch later' — cost and time to switch from a build to a vendor in year 2
'Buy → switch later' — cost and time to switch vendors or move to build in year 2
'Lock-in factors' — what makes each choice harder to reverse (data, integration depth, contract terms, organizational learning)
'One-way door / two-way door' — classification of each option
'Risk-adjusted recommendation' — given uncertainty, which option preserves more optionality
review_checklist: Switch costs are realistic, not theoretical
Lock-in includes organizational and contractual lock-in, not just technical
One-way doors warrant more caution; reversibility is a value
Risk-adjusted recommendation considers uncertainty in our future needs
prompts.prompt:
You are analyzing the reversibility of the build vs. buy choice. One-way doors warrant more caution.

Context
- Build option: {build_option}
- Buy option (vendor, contract): {buy_option}
- Time horizon: {horizon}

Produce five sections:

1. Build → switch later — cost and time to switch from a build to a vendor in year 2 if we needed to. Often surprisingly high (you've built integrations, organizational knowledge is in the build, replacing it is a project).

2. Buy → switch later — cost and time to switch vendors or move to build in year 2. Depends heavily on vendor lock-in (data portability, contract auto-renewal, integration depth).

3. Lock-in factors — for each option, what makes it harder to reverse. Technical (data portability, integration depth), contractual (auto-renewal, exclusivity), organizational (sunk cost, team has invested in learning), strategic (other systems built around it).

4. One-way door / two-way door — classification of each option. One-way doors (hard to reverse) warrant more careful upfront analysis.

5. Risk-adjusted recommendation — given uncertainty about our future needs, which option preserves more optionality? If our needs are likely to evolve, optionality is valuable. If our needs are stable, optionality is worth less.

Keep total under 600 words. Reversibility is a feature; one-way doors warrant pause.

prompts.estimated_time: 60–120 minutes per prompt; full analysis spans 1–2 weeks

prompts.title: Executive Review Prep Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for exec/QBR prep: narrative drafter, anticipated questions and risks, comp-with-other-orgs framing, and the pre-read summary.
