prompts.name: Why-this-matters stakeholder framing
prompts.use_case: Frame the issue for an exec audience that doesn't have engineering context — connect it to business outcomes they care about.
inputs: Business outcomes the audience cares about (revenue, customer trust, strategic bets)
output_format: 'Translation' — restate the issue in business terms
'Connection to their outcomes' — explicit link to revenue, customers, strategy
'Cost of inaction' — what gets worse if we don't act
'Cost of action' — what's required (eng-weeks, $, opportunity cost)
'What you might be skeptical about' — anticipated pushback with response
review_checklist: Translation is plain language; no jargon
Connection to outcomes is specific (not 'might affect customer experience')
Cost of inaction is honest, not catastrophizing
Skepticism section anticipates real pushback, not strawmen
prompts.prompt:
You are framing a technical/operational issue for a non-technical exec. Connect it to outcomes they care about.

Context
- Issue (in technical terms): {technical_issue}
- Audience: {audience}
- Business outcomes the audience cares about: {audience_outcomes}

Produce five sections:

1. Translation — restate the issue in business terms. Plain language. No jargon.

2. Connection to their outcomes — explicit link. NOT 'might affect customer experience' but 'this is causing the latency that's driving the increase in support tickets we discussed last week'.

3. Cost of inaction — what gets worse and how fast. Honest, not catastrophic.

4. Cost of action — what's required (eng-weeks, $, opportunity cost). Don't soft-pedal.

5. What you might be skeptical about — anticipated pushback with honest response. Common: 'is this just engineering wanting to refactor?', 'why now and not last quarter?', 'why can't we wait for the rewrite?'

Keep total under 500 words. Bridge from engineering to business; don't translate poorly.

prompts.name: Escalation closeout
prompts.use_case: Close out an escalation: what was decided, who owns next steps, what the team should know.
output_format: 'Decision' — what was decided, by whom, when
'Rationale' — the why, in the decision-maker's words if possible
'Action items' — table: Item | Owner | Due | First check-in
'Comms plan' — who tells whom, when
'When we'd revisit' — conditions to re-open the decision
review_checklist: Decision is named explicitly, with the decision-maker
Rationale captures the actual reasoning, not after-the-fact narrative
Action items are specific with owners
Comms plan does not skip affected stakeholders

prompts.title: Postmortem Analysis Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for blameless postmortem work: timeline reconstruction, 5-whys facilitation, action item synthesis with ownership, and cross-incident pattern detection.
prompts.why_it_matters: Postmortems either change the system or become theater. The difference is rigor at the analysis stage and discipline at the action-item stage. These prompts force both.
when_to_use: Reconstructing the incident timeline from chat logs and notes
Running 5-whys analysis on the proximate cause without slipping into blame
Synthesizing action items with owners, dates, and category
Detecting patterns across recent incidents to surface structural investments
not_for: Replacing the live postmortem meeting — these are pre/post-meeting tools
Generating shallow 5-whys that stop at the first plausible cause
source_inspiration: Sidney Dekker — The Field Guide to Understanding 'Human Error'
John Allspaw — Learning from Incidents
Google SRE — Postmortem Culture
Casey Rosenthal & Nora Jones — Chaos Engineering

inputs_required.name: Incident artifacts
inputs_required.description: Chat logs, alerts, dashboards, on-call notes, customer impact data.

inputs_required.name: Timeline boundary
inputs_required.description: Start and end times of the incident.

inputs_required.name: Recent incidents (for pattern analysis)
inputs_required.description: Last 3–5 incident summaries.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Describe behavior, not motivation.
5-whys stops at the system condition, not at 'someone made a mistake'.
Action items are owned by people, not 'the team'.

prompts.name: Incident timeline reconstruction
prompts.use_case: Reconstruct a clean timeline from raw incident artifacts (chat, alerts, dashboards) for the postmortem doc.
output_format: 'Detection' — when and how the issue was first detected
'Timeline' — chronological list with timestamp | event | source
'Decision points' — moments where the response team made a key call (and what was known at that moment)
'Resolution' — when and how the issue was mitigated; when fully resolved
'Timing metrics' — TTD (time to detect), TTM (time to mitigate), TTR (time to resolve)
review_checklist: Events are factual; speculation is marked as such
Decision points capture what was KNOWN at the moment, not what was learned later
Timing metrics are exact, not rounded to flatter the team

prompts.name: Blameless 5-whys analysis
prompts.use_case: Run a 5-whys analysis on the proximate cause that surfaces system conditions, not individual mistakes.
inputs: Surrounding system context (alerts, runbooks, on-call setup, recent changes)
What 'human error' shows up in the timeline (and what conditions enabled it)
output_format: 'Why 1' — the proximate cause and immediate condition
'Why 2' — the system condition that allowed Why 1
'Why 3' — the broader condition that allowed Why 2
'Why 4' — organizational/cultural condition that allowed Why 3
'Why 5' — root condition (often a strategic or org choice)
'Where blame slips in' — places the analysis tempted toward blaming individuals, with the structural reframe
review_checklist: Each 'why' goes deeper, not sideways
No 'why' answer is 'X person made a mistake' — that's a stop, not a why
Why 5 should be at organizational/strategic altitude
'Where blame slips in' is real (most analyses have at least one place this happened)
prompts.prompt:
You are running a blameless 5-whys analysis. The discipline: each 'why' surfaces a system condition, not a person's mistake. 'Someone made a mistake' is a stop, not a why.

Context
- Proximate cause: {proximate_cause}
- Surrounding system context: {system_context}
- Where 'human error' shows up: {human_error_appearances}

Produce six sections:

1. Why 1 — the proximate cause and the immediate condition that enabled it. Not 'engineer deployed bad config' but 'a config change with no validation was deployed because the deploy pipeline does not validate this config type'.

2. Why 2 — the system condition that allowed Why 1.

3. Why 3 — the broader condition that allowed Why 2.

4. Why 4 — organizational or cultural condition that allowed Why 3.

5. Why 5 — root condition. Often a strategic or organizational choice (e.g., 'we have not invested in deploy pipeline tooling because it has not had executive sponsor for 4 quarters').

6. Where blame slips in — places this analysis was tempted toward blaming individuals, with the structural reframe. Most analyses have at least one such place. Name them.

Keep total under 800 words. Each why goes deeper, not sideways.

prompts.name: Action items with ownership
prompts.use_case: Synthesize postmortem action items: categorize, assign owners, set dates, distinguish the must-do from the nice-to-have.
inputs: Discussion notes from the postmortem meeting
Current quarter capacity (what can realistically be funded)
output_format: 'Action items table' — Item | Category (prevent / detect / respond / resilience) | Owner | Due | Cost | Priority
'Top 3 must-do' — items that materially reduce recurrence; non-negotiable
'Should-do' — items that improve detection or response; fundable this quarter
'Nice-to-have' — items that improve resilience long-term; explicitly deferred or backlogged
'Anti-action items' — proposals that should NOT become action items (theater, blame-shifting, bandaids)
review_checklist: Owners are people, not 'the team' or 'platform'
Due dates are specific and realistic
Top 3 must-do is honest (not 5 disguised as 3)
Anti-action items section catches the failures (e.g., 'add more alerts' when the issue was alert fatigue)
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing postmortem action items from the analysis and discussion. Most postmortems list 15 actions and ship 2; this one lists fewer but ships them.

Context
- 5-whys analysis: {whys}
- Discussion notes: {discussion}
- Current quarter capacity: {capacity}

Produce five sections:

1. Action items table — Item | Category (prevent / detect / respond / resilience) | Owner (person, not team) | Due | Cost (eng-weeks) | Priority.

2. Top 3 must-do — items that materially reduce recurrence. Non-negotiable. Honest count; don't list 5 as 3.

3. Should-do — items that improve detection or response; fundable this quarter.

4. Nice-to-have — items that improve long-term resilience. Explicitly deferred or backlogged with criteria for promotion.

5. Anti-action items — proposals that should NOT become action items. Common patterns: 'add more alerts' (when the issue was alert fatigue), 'add more documentation' (when no one reads the existing docs), 'mandatory training' (when the issue was system design).

Keep total under 600 words. Fewer, real actions beat many, theater actions.

prompts.name: Cross-incident pattern detection
prompts.use_case: Detect patterns across the last 3–6 incidents to surface structural investments that one incident alone wouldn't justify.
inputs: Last 3–6 incident summaries with root causes
Service or area each incident touched
Action items from each (taken vs. deferred)
output_format: 'Pattern by area' — services/teams with multiple incidents in the period
'Pattern by cause type' — config error, deployment, dependency failure, etc.
'Pattern by response' — incidents where the response showed similar weakness
'Action items deferred multiple times' — same fix proposed in 2+ postmortems
'Structural investment recommendations' — investments that one incident wouldn't justify but the pattern does
'When the pattern would clear' — conditions under which we'd say the pattern is resolved
review_checklist: Patterns are specific (not 'we have lots of incidents')
Deferred-action callouts force the question of investment
Structural investments are concrete (a specific platform investment, an org structure change)
Clearance conditions are observable
prompts.prompt:
You are detecting patterns across recent incidents to surface structural investments that single-incident analysis misses.

Context
- Last 3–6 incident summaries with root causes: {recent_incidents}
- Services/teams each touched: {areas}
- Action items from each (taken vs. deferred): {actions_status}

Produce six sections:

1. Pattern by area — services or teams with multiple incidents in the period. Note count and severity.

2. Pattern by cause type — config error, deployment, dependency failure, capacity, observability gap, etc. Count by type.

3. Pattern by response — incidents where the response showed similar weakness (slow detection, unclear ownership, runbook missing).

4. Action items deferred multiple times — same fix proposed in 2 or more postmortems and not done. Name them; this is the most actionable signal.

5. Structural investment recommendations — investments justified by the pattern, not by any single incident. Examples: deploy pipeline validation, observability platform investment, on-call training program, service ownership clarification.

6. When the pattern would clear — observable conditions under which we'd say the pattern is resolved.

Keep total under 700 words. Patterns are the signal; single-incident analysis misses them.

prompts.title: Reliability Review Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for the reliability program: SLO/SLI proposal, error-budget burn analysis, on-call load assessment, and the quarterly reliability review.
prompts.why_it_matters: Reliability is a budget conversation, not an engineering one. Without SLOs, error budgets, and load visibility, the team optimizes for what doesn't break (yesterday) instead of what matters (this week's commitments).
when_to_use: Proposing or revising SLOs and SLIs for a service
Analyzing why error budget is burning faster than planned
Assessing on-call load (sustainability, fairness, signal-to-noise)
Drafting the quarterly reliability review for the team or exec
not_for: Inventing SLOs without service owner buy-in
Using SLO compliance as a substitute for engineering judgment
source_inspiration: Google SRE Book and Workbook
Niall Murphy / Ben Treynor / et al. on error budgets
Will Larson on on-call sustainability

inputs_required.name: Service context
inputs_required.description: What the service does, its users, current SLOs/metrics.

inputs_required.name: Recent metrics
inputs_required.description: Latency, error rate, availability over the period.

inputs_required.name: On-call data
inputs_required.description: Pages, hours, distribution across the rotation.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Reliability as budget. Sustainability as signal.
prompts: SLOs are user-facing thresholds, not internal metrics.
Error budget is for change, not just outages.
On-call load is signal about the system, not endurance.

prompts.name: SLO/SLI proposal
prompts.use_case: Propose SLOs and SLIs for a service, anchored to user experience and tied to an explicit error budget.
inputs: User experience pain points (from support, product, sales)
Comparable SLOs at peer services or competitors
output_format: 'User journey mapping' — what users do; where reliability matters most
'SLI proposals' — table: SLI | Definition | Measurement | Why this matters to users
'SLO proposals' — for each SLI, the threshold (e.g., 99.9% over 30 days)
'Error budget' — what each SLO buys: how much downtime/error per period
'What we'd give up' — explicit policies (when budget is exhausted, change freezes)
'Calibration with current data' — what % we'd hit today, which SLOs are realistic vs. aspirational
review_checklist: SLIs are user-facing (latency, error rate, throughput) not server-facing (CPU, memory)
SLOs are realistic given current data (don't propose 99.99% if you're at 99.5%)
Error budget policies are pre-committed (change freeze, additional review)
User journey map shows where reliability investment pays off
prompts.prompt:
You are proposing SLOs and SLIs for a service. Anchor in user experience; calibrate to reality.

Context
- Service description and users: {service_context}
- Current metrics: {current_metrics}
- User pain points: {pain_points}
- Peer SLOs: {peer_slos}

Produce six sections:

1. User journey mapping — what users do with this service. Identify the 2 to 3 journeys where reliability matters most.

2. SLI proposals — table: SLI | Definition | Measurement | Why this matters to users. SLIs are user-facing (latency, error rate, request success) not server-facing (CPU, RAM).

3. SLO proposals — for each SLI, the threshold (e.g., 99.9% of requests under 200ms over a rolling 30 days). Justify the threshold against user expectation.

4. Error budget — what each SLO buys: how much downtime, error, latency excess per period.

5. What we'd give up — explicit policies. When budget is exhausted: change freeze, additional review for risky deploys, mandatory reliability work.

6. Calibration with current data — what % we'd hit today. Which SLOs are realistic, which are aspirational. If aspirational, the path to closing the gap.

Keep total under 700 words. SLOs without policy are theater; include the policy.

prompts.name: Error-budget burn analysis
prompts.use_case: When the error budget is burning faster than planned, analyze why and propose what to do.
inputs: Recent incidents and changes that consumed budget
output_format: 'Burn summary' — current burn rate, projected exhaustion date if unchanged
'Sources of burn' — what consumed budget (incidents, slow deploys, capacity issues)
'Patterns' — is the burn from a single area or distributed? Recent or chronic?
'Recommended actions' — categorized: stop the bleeding | structural fix | accept and adjust
'Policy invocation' — at this burn rate, which pre-committed policies should activate?
review_checklist: Burn rate calculation is honest (not optimistic about recovery)
Sources are quantified (incident X consumed Y% of budget)
Pattern analysis distinguishes acute (one bad week) from chronic (system trend)
Policy invocation does not flinch from the pre-committed actions
prompts.prompt:
You are analyzing an error-budget burn that's faster than expected. The discipline: don't soften the analysis to avoid invoking policy.

Context
- SLO and current burn rate: {slo_burn}
- Recent incidents/changes that consumed budget: {recent_consumption}
- Upcoming planned changes: {upcoming}

Produce five sections:

1. Burn summary — current burn rate, projected exhaustion date if rate continues. Honest projection; don't assume self-recovery.

2. Sources of burn — what consumed budget. Quantified: 'incident X on Y date consumed 40% of monthly budget'. List the contributors.

3. Patterns — is the burn from a single area or distributed? Recent (acute) or chronic (system trend)? This determines the right intervention.

4. Recommended actions, categorized: Stop the bleeding (immediate) | Structural fix (this quarter) | Accept and adjust (revise SLO if it was wrong).

5. Policy invocation — at this burn rate, which pre-committed policies should activate? (Change freeze, additional review, mandatory reliability work.) Don't flinch from invoking what was pre-committed.

Keep total under 600 words. The policy works only if it's invoked when the trigger fires.

prompts.name: On-call load assessment
prompts.use_case: Assess on-call sustainability: hours, wake-ups, signal-to-noise, fairness across the rotation.
inputs: On-call data over last 4–8 weeks (pages, hours, after-hours)
output_format: 'Load metrics' — average pages per shift, after-hours pages, hours of attention
'Distribution' — who's getting paged most, fairness across rotation
'Signal-to-noise' — % of pages that required immediate action vs. could have waited or were noise
'Sustainability flags' — wake-ups per week, weekend pages, recovery time
'Recommendations' — categorized: cut alerts (noise) | improve runbooks | route differently | invest in self-healing | rebalance rotation
review_checklist: Load metrics include after-hours and weekend, not just total count
Distribution analysis names individuals if there's an imbalance (for the manager's prep, not for public)
Signal-to-noise drives the highest-leverage recommendations
Recommendations are specific (not 'reduce noise')
prompts.prompt:
You are assessing on-call load and sustainability for a rotation. The metric that matters most is signal-to-noise; everything else flows from it.

Context
- On-call data (4–8 weeks): {oncall_data}
- Rotation membership and frequency: {rotation}
- Burnout/attrition signals: {burnout_signals}

Produce five sections:

1. Load metrics — average pages per shift, after-hours pages (nights/weekends), total hours of attention per shift. Include the worst week, not just average.

2. Distribution — who's getting paged most. Fairness analysis. If there's an imbalance, name it (for manager prep).

3. Signal-to-noise — % of pages that required immediate action vs. could have waited until business hours vs. were noise/false-positive. This is the highest-leverage metric.

4. Sustainability flags — wake-ups per week, weekend pages, recovery time between shifts. If any engineer is doing >2 wake-ups per week sustained, flag it.

5. Recommendations, categorized: cut alerts (noise) | improve runbooks (response time) | route differently (not page on-call) | invest in self-healing | rebalance rotation. Specific actions, not 'reduce noise'.

Keep total under 600 words. Sustainability is a structural problem, not an endurance problem.

prompts.name: Quarterly reliability review
prompts.use_case: Draft the quarterly reliability review for the team and one tier up: SLO compliance, incident summary, on-call sustainability, what we changed, what's next.
output_format: 'Headline' — 2 sentences capturing the state of reliability
'SLO compliance' — table per service: target | actual | budget remaining
'Incident summary' — count by severity, total impact, top 3 lessons
'On-call sustainability' — load metrics with trend
'Investments completed this quarter' — what we did, what changed
'Investments planned next quarter' — with rationale tied to the data above
'Open risks' — what's worrying us going forward
review_checklist: Headline doesn't oversell ('all green' when budget is at 30%)
Incident summary names lessons, not 'no recurring issues'
On-call trend is honest (improving or worsening, with cause)
Investments planned are tied to the data, not generic
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting the quarterly reliability review. Audience: team plus one tier of leadership. Honest about the state, structured about what's next.

Context
- SLO compliance: {slo_compliance}
- Incident summary for the quarter: {incident_summary}
- On-call load data: {oncall_data}
- Reliability investments (done and planned): {investments}

Produce seven sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences capturing the state of reliability. Don't oversell.

2. SLO compliance — table per service: SLO target | actual | error budget remaining (% and time-to-exhaustion if applicable).

3. Incident summary — count by severity, total customer impact, top 3 lessons (not 'no recurring issues' — name what we learned).

4. On-call sustainability — load metrics (pages, after-hours, hours) with trend (improving / worsening / stable, with cause).

5. Investments completed this quarter — what we did, what observably changed.

6. Investments planned next quarter — with rationale tied to the data above. NOT 'continue reliability work' but 'reduce on-call noise by retiring 14 alerts identified in Q2 burn analysis'.

7. Open risks — what's worrying us going forward.

Keep total under 800 words. Honest beats green.

prompts.title: Risk Register Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for risk management: identification facilitation, scoring (impact × probability), mitigation planning, and quarterly register review.
prompts.why_it_matters: Risk registers either drive investment or become a list nobody reads. The difference is rigor at scoring and discipline at mitigation. These prompts force both.
when_to_use: Facilitating risk identification with the team or partners
Scoring identified risks for prioritization
Drafting mitigation plans with owners and timing
Quarterly review of the existing register: what changed, what to add, what to retire
not_for: Generating risks without team input — these refine, not invent
Using risk scoring as a substitute for judgment
source_inspiration: Standard enterprise risk management frameworks (ISO 31000)
Will Larson on systemic risk in engineering orgs
John Allspaw — Learning from Incidents on latent risk

inputs_required.name: Scope of register
inputs_required.description: What surface area does this register cover (service, team, org).

inputs_required.name: Recent risk signals
inputs_required.description: Incidents, near-misses, audit findings, dependency changes.

inputs_required.name: Existing register
inputs_required.description: Current entries if you're updating; empty if new.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Specific risks. Honest scoring. Owned mitigations.
prompts: A risk is a future event with a probability and an impact, not a problem.
Scoring without anchors becomes ritual.
Mitigation requires an owner and a date, or it's wishful.

prompts.name: Risk identification facilitation
prompts.use_case: Facilitate a risk identification session with the team — surface risks across categories, not just the obvious technical ones.
inputs: Major changes in flight (people, tech, dependencies)
output_format: 'Risk categories' — operational | strategic | dependency | people | financial | regulatory | security
'Identified risks per category' — list with one-line description
'Latent risks' — risks not yet visible but suggested by patterns
'Out-of-scope risks' — risks the team named but that belong elsewhere
'Discussion prompts' — questions to surface risks the team hasn't raised
review_checklist: Risks are events, not problems ('major contributor leaves the team in next 6 months' not 'team is too small')
All categories represented — most teams over-index on operational, miss strategic and people
Latent risks include 'we haven't tested X' or 'we depend on Y team's commitment'
Discussion prompts surface unspoken risks

prompts.name: Risk scoring (impact × probability)
prompts.use_case: Score identified risks with explicit anchors so scoring isn't ritual.
inputs: Scoring scale anchors (what counts as low/medium/high impact and probability)
output_format: 'Scoring rubric' — explicit anchors for impact (low/medium/high/critical) and probability (rare/possible/likely/expected)
'Scored register' — table: Risk | Impact | Probability | Score | Velocity (getting worse?) | Owner-area
'Top quintile risks' — the 20% that warrant active mitigation
'Watch list' — risks below the line but with worrying velocity
'Calibration notes' — places where scoring required judgment, with rationale
review_checklist: Anchors are concrete (e.g., 'critical impact = customer-visible outage > 4 hours')
Velocity column flags risks that are getting worse, not just current state
Top quintile is small enough to actually mitigate (not 'top 60%')
Calibration notes capture the judgment calls
prompts.prompt:
You are scoring identified risks. Without explicit anchors, scoring becomes ritual.

Context
- Identified risks: {risks}
- Scoring anchors (or write your own): {anchors}
- Time horizon: {horizon}

Produce five sections:

1. Scoring rubric — explicit anchors. Impact: Low (minor inconvenience) / Medium (degraded service for some users) / High (major customer impact, SLO breach) / Critical (regulatory, safety, business existence). Probability: Rare (<5% in horizon) / Possible (5–25%) / Likely (25–60%) / Expected (>60%).

2. Scored register — table: Risk | Impact | Probability | Score (Impact × Probability) | Velocity (getting worse / stable / improving) | Owner-area.

3. Top quintile risks — the top 20% by score. Small enough to actually mitigate. Don't list 60%.

4. Watch list — risks below the line but with worrying velocity. They're not top-quintile today but might be next quarter.

5. Calibration notes — places scoring required judgment. For each: the call you made and why. This protects the scoring from second-guessing.

Keep total under 600 words. Anchored scoring beats ritual scoring.

prompts.name: Mitigation planning
prompts.use_case: For top-quintile risks, draft mitigation plans with owners, dates, and acceptance criteria.
output_format: 'Mitigation matrix per risk' — Risk | Existing controls | Gap | Mitigation actions | Owner | Date | Acceptance criteria
'Mitigation categories' — eliminate / reduce probability / reduce impact / transfer / accept
'Accepted risks' — risks we are explicitly accepting (with rationale)
'Funded vs. unfunded mitigations' — which we can do this quarter, which need investment we don't have
review_checklist: Owner is a person, not a team
Date is realistic given capacity
Acceptance criteria are observable ('alert configured and tested under failure scenario X' not 'monitoring improved')
Accepted risks are explicit and signed off
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting mitigation plans for top-quintile risks. Mitigation without owner-date-criteria is wishful.

Context
- Top-quintile risks: {top_risks}
- Available capacity for mitigation: {capacity}
- Existing controls: {existing_controls}

Produce four sections:

1. Mitigation matrix per risk — for each risk: Existing controls | Gap | Mitigation actions | Owner (person) | Date (specific) | Acceptance criteria (observable).

2. Mitigation categories — for each risk, classify the strategy: eliminate (remove the risk entirely), reduce probability, reduce impact, transfer (insurance, contractual), or accept.

3. Accepted risks — risks we are explicitly accepting. With rationale. Signed off by appropriate altitude (a high-impact accepted risk should be exec-signed).

4. Funded vs. unfunded mitigations — which we can do this quarter, which require investment we don't have. The unfunded ones are the asks for next quarter or higher altitude.

Keep total under 700 words. Mitigations with owners ship; without owners they don't.

prompts.name: Quarterly register review
prompts.use_case: Quarterly review of the risk register: what changed, what to add (new risks), what to retire (resolved or no longer relevant).
output_format: 'Risks closed' — mitigated and now resolved, with evidence
'Risks unchanged' — still active, no material change
'Risks worsened' — increased in impact or probability, with cause
'New risks added' — risks identified this quarter
'Risks retired' — no longer applicable (e.g., service decommissioned)
'Top movers' — risks that changed quintile (up or down)
'Headline for the audience' — 2 sentences capturing register state
review_checklist: Closed risks have evidence ('mitigation X completed, observed under failure scenario Y')
Worsened risks name the cause
Retired risks are explicit (don't just disappear)
Headline is honest about overall state
prompts.prompt:
You are reviewing the risk register for the quarter. The register is alive when it changes; dead when it doesn't.

Context
- Current register: {current_register}
- Mitigations completed this quarter: {completed_mitigations}
- Recent incidents/near-misses: {recent_incidents}
- Strategic shifts: {strategic_shifts}

Produce seven sections:

1. Risks closed — mitigated and now resolved. Each with evidence (the mitigation completed, observed working).

2. Risks unchanged — still active, no material change. Brief; don't dwell.

3. Risks worsened — increased in impact or probability since last review. Name the cause (new dependency, attrition, scope expansion).

4. New risks added — risks identified this quarter from incidents, near-misses, or shifts.

5. Risks retired — no longer applicable (service decommissioned, dependency removed). Explicit; don't let them silently disappear.

6. Top movers — risks that changed quintile (up or down). Note the change and the cause.

7. Headline for the audience — 2 sentences capturing register state. Honest about overall trend.

Keep total under 700 words. Live register beats static one.

prompts.title: Future-State Design Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for target operating model design: Conway's Law diagnosis, target topology draft, current-to-future migration plan, and anti-pattern stress-test.
prompts.why_it_matters: Org design is the most expensive tool a leader has — and the easiest to misuse. Good designs align team boundaries with the product and communication graph. Bad ones create friction that shows up as slow delivery, reliability issues, and attrition.
when_to_use: Designing a target operating model for an org
Diagnosing misalignment via Conway's Law
Planning the migration from current to future state
Stress-testing a proposed design for common anti-patterns
not_for: Using reorgs to solve problems that aren't structural (performance, strategy, leadership)
Replacing conversation with the affected people — org design is done WITH the org, not TO it
source_inspiration: Matthew Skelton & Manuel Pais — Team Topologies
Conway's Law original paper
Spotify model (and its critiques)
Will Larson on systems of systems

inputs_required.name: Current state
inputs_required.description: Current org structure, teams, ownership.

inputs_required.name: Strategic context
inputs_required.description: What the business needs next 12–24 months.

inputs_required.name: Pain points
inputs_required.description: What isn't working in the current design.

inputs_required.name: Constraints
inputs_required.description: Headcount, timing, leadership availability, cultural constraints.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Align team boundaries with product and communication graph.
prompts: If team X and team Y need to talk daily, they should be one team or have a clear interaction mode.
Every team type (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem) has a distinct purpose.
Anti-patterns: matrix orgs, shared services, too many cooks.

prompts.name: Conway's Law diagnosis
prompts.use_case: Diagnose current org against Conway's Law — where is the team structure creating friction in the product or architecture?
inputs: Current team structure (org chart + ownership map)
Known friction points (slow features, integration issues, duplicate work)
output_format: 'Current graph' — teams and their primary communication partners
'Architecture graph' — system components and their interaction
'Alignment analysis' — where team structure matches architecture (healthy) and where it doesn't (friction)
'Inverse Conway opportunities' — places where we could reshape teams to enable the architecture we want
'Forced communication paths' — times when teams must interact across boundaries, with the cost of that communication
review_checklist: Current graph reflects actual working relationships, not the org chart
Friction points are specific (not 'communication issues')
Inverse Conway opportunities name concrete team moves
Cost of forced communication is quantified if possible (meetings, delay, rework)
prompts.prompt:
You are diagnosing the org against Conway's Law. The question: does team structure match the communication graph the architecture requires?

Context
- Current team structure: {current_structure}
- Product/system architecture: {architecture}
- Known friction points: {friction}

Produce five sections:

1. Current graph — teams and their primary communication partners. Based on actual working relationships (who talks to whom weekly), not the org chart.

2. Architecture graph — system components and their interaction patterns. Which components call which? Which share data?

3. Alignment analysis — where team structure matches architecture (healthy: the team that owns component A has low coordination cost with team that owns component B if they rarely interact). Where doesn't it (friction: teams forced to coordinate constantly through escalation or standing meetings).

4. Inverse Conway opportunities — places where we could reshape teams to ENABLE the architecture we want. Specific team moves.

5. Forced communication paths — times teams must interact across boundaries. Quantify the cost where possible (meetings per week, delay per handoff, rework from misaligned priorities).

Keep total under 700 words. Diagnosis-led; don't jump to solutions.

prompts.name: Target topology draft
prompts.use_case: Draft a target topology using Team Topologies patterns (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem) with explicit interaction modes.
output_format: 'Team inventory' — for each proposed team: Type | Mission | Scope | Size | Interaction modes
'Team types mix' — why these four types in these proportions?
'Interaction modes' — collaboration, X-as-a-Service, facilitating. Explicit pairs, explicit purpose
'Coverage analysis' — what product/system areas each team covers; any gaps or overlaps
'Capability gaps' — what the target state requires that current people don't have yet
'Size ceilings' — max size per team with rationale (typically 5–9)
review_checklist: Each team has a clear mission (not 'work on platform')
Interaction modes are explicit (collaborating with whom, service to whom)
Coverage has no major gaps or overlaps
Size ceilings honor the Dunbar-ish constraints
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting a target topology for the org using Team Topologies patterns. Team types, interaction modes, and cognitive-load limits are the discipline.

Context
- Diagnosis: {diagnosis}
- Strategic context: {strategic_context}
- Headcount budget: {headcount}
- Capability gaps: {gaps}

Produce six sections:

1. Team inventory — for each proposed team: Type (stream-aligned / platform / enabling / complicated-subsystem) | Mission | Scope | Size | Interaction modes with other teams.

2. Team types mix — why this proportion? Typically ~60–70% stream-aligned, the rest platform/enabling/complicated-subsystem.

3. Interaction modes — explicit pairs: Team A collaborates with Team B on X; Team C provides X-as-a-Service to D, E, F; Team G facilitates H for a bounded period.

4. Coverage analysis — what product/system areas each team covers. Flag gaps (no one owns X) and overlaps (two teams both own Y).

5. Capability gaps — what the target state requires that current people don't have yet. New manager hires, skill gaps, specialized roles.

6. Size ceilings — max size per team (typically 5–9) with rationale tied to cognitive load.

Keep total under 900 words. Patterns over invention.

prompts.name: Current-to-future migration plan
prompts.use_case: Plan the migration from current org to target: sequencing, comms, risk, and the explicit interim states.
output_format: 'Migration sequence' — phases with dates and what changes in each
'Interim states' — what the org looks like during migration (not just start and end)
'Risk table' — risks per phase with mitigations
'Comms plan' — who tells whom, when, in what order
'Readiness assessment' — who's ready to lead which new team, who needs development
'Go/no-go gates' — conditions that must hold to proceed to the next phase
review_checklist: Interim states are real (most migrations live in interim for months)
Comms plan does not surprise affected people (they hear from their manager first, not in a company-wide email)
Readiness assessment is honest; don't pretend everyone is ready
Go/no-go gates prevent bad migrations from barreling through
prompts.prompt:
You are planning the migration from current org to target topology. Most reorgs fail at the interim state and the comms sequencing.

Context
- Current state: {current}
- Target topology: {target}
- Timing constraints: {timing}
- Affected leaders and readiness: {leader_readiness}

Produce six sections:

1. Migration sequence — phases with dates. What changes in each phase. Typically 3 to 5 phases over 2 to 6 months.

2. Interim states — what the org looks like during migration. Not just start and end. Most migrations live in interim for months; the interim state has to work.

3. Risk table — per phase: key risks and mitigations.

4. Comms plan — who tells whom, in what order. Affected people hear from their manager first; the company-wide announcement comes after 1:1s. Cascade plan explicit.

5. Readiness assessment — who's ready to lead which new team today? Who needs development? Who should not be in the new structure? Honest.

6. Go/no-go gates — conditions that must hold to proceed to the next phase. Examples: 'lead for Team X identified and accepted', 'key dependency team aligned on interface'.

Keep total under 800 words. Sequencing and comms; don't rush.

prompts.name: Anti-pattern stress-test
prompts.use_case: Stress-test the target topology against common org-design anti-patterns. Most bad designs are recognizable in advance.
inputs: Known anti-patterns in this company's history
output_format: 'Anti-pattern check' — for each common anti-pattern: does our design risk this? Why / why not?
'Matrix org risk' — do any people report to more than one leader?
'Shared services death spiral' — any 'platform' teams with no clear accountability?
'Too many cooks' — any decisions requiring 5+ people's approval?
'Layering' — any 4+ layer structures that add management overhead without value?
'Spans of control' — any manager with >8 direct reports or <3?
'Recommended adjustments' — specific changes to avoid predicted failures
review_checklist: Each anti-pattern check is honest (no 'our design avoids this' without evidence)
Shared services check includes accountability mechanism (backlog, SLA, roadmap)
Span-of-control check names specific people who'd be out of range
Recommended adjustments are specific
prompts.prompt:
You are stress-testing a target topology against common org-design anti-patterns. Most bad designs are recognizable in advance.

Context
- Target topology: {target}
- Known anti-patterns in this company's history: {history}
- Peer org horror stories to avoid: {peer_stories}

Produce seven sections, each honest:

1. Anti-pattern check (overall) — for each common anti-pattern: does our design risk it? Why or why not?

2. Matrix org risk — do any people report to more than one leader? Dotted lines often become real reports in practice.

3. Shared services death spiral — any 'platform' teams with no clear accountability mechanism (backlog, SLA, roadmap)? Platform teams without a product discipline become dumping grounds.

4. Too many cooks — any decisions requiring 5+ people's approval? Decision velocity dies there.

5. Layering — any 4+ layer structures that add management overhead without value? (IC → manager → manager-of-managers → director → VP for a 40-person org is too many.)

6. Spans of control — any manager with more than 8 direct reports (under-support) or fewer than 3 (over-management, cost)?

7. Recommended adjustments — specific changes to avoid predicted failures. Not 'consider reducing layers' but 'remove the tech-lead-manager layer in team X and promote two leads to report directly'.

Keep total under 800 words. Adversarial in service of the design.

prompts.title: Layoff Comms Drafter Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for layoff communication: manager 1:1 script with affected person, team comms within 1 hour, all-hands Q&A prep, and surviving-team check-in cadence.
prompts.why_it_matters: Layoffs are the most consequential people action a company takes. The decision cannot be undone in the comms; the comms can only honor it well or compound the damage. These prompts produce the language under conditions where the writer has no spare attention.
when_to_use: Drafting the manager-led 1:1 script with affected people (the day-of, most consequential conversation)
Drafting the team comms delivered within 1 hour of the last 1:1
Preparing for the all-hands Q&A 24–48 hours later
Designing the surviving-team check-in cadence for the next 30 days
not_for: Replacing legal / HR partner consultation — every step requires their partnership
Deciding whether to do the layoff (separate workstream)
Bypassing PR/Comms on external messaging
source_inspiration: Patty McCord — Powerful, on radical clarity
Lara Hogan — layoff comms blog posts
First Round Review — layoff execution interviews

inputs_required.name: Layoff scope and rationale
inputs_required.description: Number affected, the reason at the right altitude, severance/support package.

inputs_required.name: Affected person context (for the 1:1 script)
inputs_required.description: Name, role, tenure, recent performance status (typically not the criterion in true layoffs).

inputs_required.name: HR partner aligned
inputs_required.description: HR involved in every step.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: The kindness is in the clarity.
Do not over-apologize or over-explain.
Remember the surviving team.

prompts.name: Affected-person 1:1 script
prompts.use_case: Draft the manager-led 1:1 script for telling an affected person their role is being eliminated. Most consequential conversation in the cycle.
inputs: Manager's relationship history with the person
output_format: 'Pre-conversation prep' — manager's own preparation
'Opening' — verbatim first 60 seconds
'The decision' — verbatim
'Severance and support' — verbatim summary
'Logistics' — what happens next
'Closing' — manager's personal commitment
'During-conversation rules' — listen, don't negotiate, don't bad-mouth, etc.
review_checklist: Opening doesn't start with chitchat — affected person knows in first 30 seconds
Decision is named clearly as final, not a discussion
Reason is at the right altitude (business decision, not performance)
Severance summary is in writing, available immediately after
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing a manager to deliver a layoff to an affected person. Hardest conversation; the kindness is in the clarity.

Context
- Affected person: {affected_person}
- Severance package summary: {severance}
- Logistics: {logistics}
- Manager's relationship history: {relationship}

Produce seven sections:

1. Pre-conversation prep — manager's own preparation. HR partner briefed and available. Severance packet ready in writing. Office logistics planned. Script reviewed. Manager has done their own emotional processing in advance — don't process it in the conversation.

2. Opening (verbatim, first 60 seconds) — '[Name], thank you for making time. I have hard news to share. Your role is being eliminated as part of a workforce reduction we're announcing today. This decision is final. I want to walk you through what's happening and what support we're providing.'

3. The decision (verbatim) — 'The company is reducing the [team/function/org] by [scope] effective [date]. This was a business decision based on [one-line reason: financial pressure / strategic shift / restructuring]. It is not a reflection of your performance or your contributions to this team.'

4. Severance and support (verbatim summary) — last day, severance amount, healthcare continuation, outplacement, equity treatment. HR partner is the contact for package questions.

5. Logistics — equipment return, final paychecks, references, written details emailed within the hour.

6. Closing (verbatim) — manager's personal commitment: 'I am going to be a reference for you. I will write you a recommendation. If there's anything I can do — introductions, advice, just to talk in a few weeks — please reach out. I'm sorry. This is hard, and I want you to know I see how hard.'

7. During-conversation rules — listen more than you talk; do not over-explain or apologize repeatedly; do not negotiate (decision is final); do not project optimism for them; answer questions honestly ('I don't know' acceptable when true); do not bad-mouth the company or the decision.

Keep total under 800 words. Direct, warm, specific.

prompts.name: Team comms script (within 1 hour)
prompts.use_case: Draft the team-level comms the manager delivers to the remaining team within 1 hour of the last 1:1. Voice, not Slack.
inputs: Specific things to honor about the people leaving
output_format: 'Format' — in-person team meeting (or video if remote)
'Opening' — verbatim
'The why' — verbatim at the right altitude
'Honoring departed colleagues' — verbatim
'For people staying' — verbatim
'Closing' — verbatim
'Tone and don'ts'
review_checklist: Opening doesn't lead with what's exciting; honor the moment of loss
Why is honest at the right altitude
Departed colleagues honored specifically by name and contribution
What's true for those staying is concrete (not 'continue great work')
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing a manager to deliver team-level comms within 1 hour of the last 1:1. Voice, not Slack. The team will know something is wrong; don't leave them to speculate.

Context
- Team makeup (who's leaving, who's staying): {team_makeup}
- Layoff scope at company level: {company_scope}
- Things to honor about people leaving (specific contributions): {departed_contributions}
- What's true for those staying (work, role, scope): {staying_context}

Produce seven sections:

1. Format — in-person team meeting (or video if remote). NOT Slack.

2. Opening (verbatim) — 'I have hard news. Today we made workforce reductions affecting [scope]. On our team, [specific names]: are no longer with us as of today. I have already met with each of them individually. They are receiving severance, healthcare continuation, and outplacement support.'

3. The why (verbatim at the right altitude) — 'This decision was made because [honest reason: company is restructuring [function/area] in response to [strategic shift / financial reality]]. It is not a reflection of the work this team has done.'

4. Honoring departed colleagues (verbatim) — 'I want to take a moment to acknowledge [names] and what they contributed. [Specific things — a project, a quality, an impact]. They are good people who did good work. They deserve our gratitude.'

5. For people staying (verbatim) — 'I know this raises questions. Here's what I can tell you: [Decisions affecting our team are complete; there are no further reductions planned (only if true)]. [The work we own is continuing as-is / shifting in these specific ways]. [Your roles are secure / changing in these specific ways]. I will be available for 1:1s starting [time] for anyone who wants to talk.'

6. Closing (verbatim) — 'If you want to reach out to [departed colleagues] personally, please do — they are not leaving because of anything they did wrong. Their contact info will be in the email I send shortly. I am here. I will be here. Take the time you need this afternoon.'

7. Tone and don'ts — do not lead with what's exciting going forward (this is a moment of loss; honor it). Do not promise no further layoffs unless you genuinely know. Do not minimize. Do not over-promise the future.

Keep total under 800 words. Honor the moment; honest about what's next.

prompts.name: All-hands Q&A prep
prompts.use_case: Prep the engineering exec for the all-hands Q&A 24–48 hours after the layoff. Anonymous questions accepted; honest answers expected.
inputs: What HR allows / requires you to share or not
output_format: 'Top 10 likely questions' — with honest answers
'The hardest question' — the one you don't want to answer; how to handle
'Questions you can't answer' — what to say when the honest answer is 'I can't share'
'Pre-submitted questions' — synthesis and answers
'Tone notes' — take questions live, 'I don't know' acceptable, don't perform optimism
review_checklist: Honest answers throughout; do not soft-pedal
'Are there more layoffs coming?' has an honest answer (don't promise what you don't know)
Hardest question is named and addressed
'I can't share' is used carefully, with reason
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing the engineering exec for the all-hands Q&A 24–48 hours after a layoff. The room is hurting and watching for honesty.

Context
- Layoff scope and rationale: {scope_rationale}
- Anticipated tough questions: {tough_questions}
- What you're permitted to share / not share (HR-aligned): {permission}
- Pre-submitted anonymous questions: {pre_submitted}

Produce five sections:

1. Top 10 likely questions with honest answers. Common questions:
   - 'Are there more layoffs coming?' — honest; do not promise what you don't know
   - 'Why these people specifically?' — at the right altitude (role redundancy, function consolidation, scope shift); not by name
   - 'Why not pay cuts / hiring freeze / slower growth?' — honest about why this option was chosen
   - 'What happens to [project / team]?' — specific
   - 'How are [departed colleagues] doing?' — honest, respectful
   - 'Are leadership taking pay cuts too?' — honest answer
   - 'How is morale going to recover?' — honest acknowledgment that recovery takes time

2. The hardest question — the one you don't want to answer (e.g., 'did you fight for these roles?', 'why did the company hire so much last year?'). How to handle: acknowledge directly, answer honestly, don't deflect.

3. Questions you can't answer — what to say when honest answer is 'I can't share for legal/privacy reasons'. Be explicit about why, not vague.

4. Pre-submitted questions — synthesis (group similar) and proposed answers.

5. Tone notes — take questions live (don't filter to softballs); 'I don't know' is acceptable when true; 'I can't share that' is acceptable when true (explain why); don't perform optimism — the room will see through it.

Keep total under 1000 words. Honest beats polished.

prompts.name: Surviving-team check-in cadence
prompts.use_case: Design the 30-day check-in cadence for the surviving team. Long-term damage from layoffs is on the people who stay.
inputs: Specific concerns expressed in 1:1s and Slack
What's changing for the team's work
output_format: 'Week 1 cadence' — 1:1 with every direct report; open office hours; update on work
'Weeks 2–4 cadence' — continuous 1:1s; visible manager presence; concrete forward motion
'Day 30 pulse-check' — anonymous survey; synthesis; visible response
'What to watch for' — disengagement, attrition risk, performance dips
'What NOT to do' — common manager failure modes during recovery period
review_checklist: Week 1 cadence is intensive (every report, ask 'how are you holding up')
Weeks 2–4 stays intentional; manager doesn't retreat
Day 30 pulse leads to visible response, not just a survey
Watch-fors include concrete signals
prompts.prompt:
You are designing the 30-day check-in cadence for the surviving team after a layoff. Long-term damage is on the people who stay.

Context
- Team size and composition post-layoff: {team_post_layoff}
- Specific concerns expressed: {concerns}
- What's changing for the team's work: {work_changes}

Produce five sections:

1. Week 1 cadence — 1:1 with every direct report; open ask 'how are you holding up?'; open office hours for cross-team conversations; update on what work continues, shifts, stops; honest about what you don't know yet.

2. Weeks 2–4 cadence — continue 1:1 cadence; watch for signs of disengagement; be visible (do not retreat to your calendar); recommit to specific work to give the team forward motion.

3. Day 30 pulse-check — anonymous survey on how people are feeling, what they need, what should be done differently. Synthesize and respond — don't survey without acting. Visible response in week 5.

4. What to watch for — disengagement (less participation in standups, retros), attrition risk (1:1 sentiment, LinkedIn updates), performance dips (commitment slippage, code quality), survivor's guilt, manager-of-manager bypass (escalating to your boss because they don't trust you).

5. What NOT to do — common manager failures during recovery: retreat to your calendar; perform 'business as usual' too quickly; promise no more layoffs without knowing; let surface-level 'fine' substitute for real check-in; survey without acting on results; cut 1:1 cadence after week 2.

Keep total under 700 words. Survivors deserve active management; recovery takes weeks, not days.

prompts.estimated_time: 60–120 minutes per prompt; layoff comms execute over 24–48 hours; surviving-team work over 30+ days

prompts.title: Leadership Cadence Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for leadership operating cadence: meeting audit, cadence designer, skip-level rhythm, and async-vs-sync rebalance.
prompts.why_it_matters: A leader's calendar is the org's operating system. Most cadences accrete meetings over time until they consume all the discretionary thinking time. These prompts force intentional design.
when_to_use: Auditing a crowded calendar to surface what can be cut
Designing a new leadership cadence (for a newly-promoted director or VP)
Establishing or reshaping the skip-level rhythm
Rebalancing the async-vs-sync ratio when the team is burning out on meetings
not_for: Cutting meetings unilaterally without considering the signal each produces
Creating async workflows that require synchronous explanation to work
source_inspiration: Paul Graham — Maker's Schedule / Manager's Schedule
Amazon — meeting discipline and pre-reads
GitLab Handbook — async-first operating norms
Lara Hogan — meeting audit frameworks

inputs_required.name: Current calendar
inputs_required.description: Recurring meetings in a typical week with purpose and participants.

inputs_required.name: Scope of leadership
inputs_required.description: Team size, reports, peer partners.

inputs_required.name: Pain points
inputs_required.description: What's not working: too many meetings, decisions slow, info flow broken.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Every meeting produces a signal or a decision — or it's dead.
Cadence should match the work: daily for active incidents, weekly for execution, monthly for direction.
Async-by-default frees the best thinking time.

prompts.name: Meeting audit
prompts.use_case: Audit a crowded leadership calendar — what to cut, what to consolidate, what's missing.
inputs: Current recurring meetings (name, cadence, duration, participants, purpose)
What each meeting produces (decision / signal / alignment)
output_format: 'Meeting inventory' — table with all recurring meetings and weekly hour cost
'Value classification' — per meeting: produces decision / produces signal / produces alignment / produces nothing detectable
'Cut candidates' — meetings that produce nothing detectable or can be async
'Consolidate candidates' — overlapping meetings that could merge
'Missing meetings' — decisions or signals not currently happening anywhere
'Post-audit calendar' — proposed state with hour savings
review_checklist: Value classification is honest (most leaders have at least one meeting that produces nothing)
Cut candidates consider the signal the meeting surfaces, not just the decision
Missing meetings section is real (often missing: skip-levels, 1:1 with peers, strategy time)
Hour savings are quantified
prompts.prompt:
You are auditing a leadership calendar. The question is not 'can this meeting be shorter' but 'does this meeting exist'.

Context
- Current recurring meetings: {meetings}
- What each produces: {meeting_outputs}
- Leader's role: {role}

Produce six sections:

1. Meeting inventory — table of all recurring meetings: Name | Cadence | Duration | Participants | Purpose | Weekly hour cost.

2. Value classification — per meeting: produces decision / produces signal / produces alignment / produces nothing detectable. Be honest; most crowded calendars have at least one meeting in the last category.

3. Cut candidates — meetings that produce nothing detectable OR could be async. Consider the signal the meeting surfaces (visibility, relationship, informal coordination) even if no decision is made.

4. Consolidate candidates — meetings that overlap in purpose and could merge.

5. Missing meetings — decisions or signals not currently happening anywhere. Often missing: skip-levels, 1:1 with peer leaders, dedicated strategic thinking time.

6. Post-audit calendar — proposed state with the hour savings quantified.

Keep total under 700 words. Every meeting defended, or cut.

prompts.name: Leadership cadence designer
prompts.use_case: Design a leadership cadence from scratch — what rhythms, at what frequencies, for what outcomes.
inputs: Key relationships (reports, peers, exec, cross-functional partners)
What the leader needs to see / decide / produce
output_format: 'Daily rhythms' — what happens every day
'Weekly rhythms' — what happens each week (1:1s, staff meetings, peer syncs)
'Monthly rhythms' — monthly-cadence items (skip-levels, deep-dives, cross-functional)
'Quarterly rhythms' — QBR, planning, off-site, strategic reviews
'Ad-hoc allocation' — how much time reserved for unplanned work (typically 20–30%)
'What this cadence optimizes for' — the trade-off this cadence makes vs. alternatives
review_checklist: Cadence matches the work (daily for active incidents, weekly for execution, monthly for direction)
Ad-hoc allocation is explicit (20–30% healthy)
What this optimizes for is honest (this cadence is great for X but weak on Y)
1:1s appear at appropriate frequencies (weekly for reports, biweekly for peers, monthly for skips)
prompts.prompt:
You are designing a leadership cadence. The calendar is the org's operating system; design it intentionally.

Context
- Leader's role and scope: {role_scope}
- Key relationships: {relationships}
- What the leader needs to see / decide / produce: {outputs}

Produce six sections:

1. Daily rhythms — what happens every day. Typically: calendar review, async inbox triage, short stand-ups during active work, end-of-day triage.

2. Weekly rhythms — what happens each week. 1:1s with direct reports, staff meeting, peer sync, cross-functional check-in.

3. Monthly rhythms — skip-levels, deep-dives on specific programs, cross-functional reviews, team all-hands.

4. Quarterly rhythms — QBR, planning kickoff, off-site, strategic review.

5. Ad-hoc allocation — reserved capacity for unplanned work: incidents, urgent decisions, strategic thinking. 20–30% is healthy; less is brittle.

6. What this cadence optimizes for — honest trade-off. This cadence is great for X but weak on Y. Name the weakness so you can mitigate.

Keep total under 700 words. Intentional over accreted.

prompts.name: Skip-level rhythm design
prompts.use_case: Design the skip-level rhythm: frequency, format, topics, what to do with what you hear.
inputs: Goals of skip-levels (people signal / team health / direct IC feedback / unblock)
Current challenges (stale managers, weak signal, no time)
output_format: 'Cadence' — frequency per person, duration per session, reset cadence
'Format' — agenda structure, who leads, confidentiality norms
'Topics' — standing topics vs. rotating
'What to do with signal' — how skip-level info flows back (or not) to the manager between them and the skip-level report
'Anti-patterns' — things to avoid (spying on managers, solving problems that go around the manager)
review_checklist: Cadence is sustainable (a VP with 40 skip-level reports can't meet each monthly)
Confidentiality norms are explicit
Signal-back-to-manager path is clear (when do you tell the manager, when don't you)
Anti-patterns are named (this is the area where skip-levels most often go wrong)
prompts.prompt:
You are designing a skip-level rhythm. Skip-levels produce signal about managers and teams; done wrong they produce distrust.

Context
- Number of skip-level reports: {skip_count}
- Goals of skip-levels: {goals}
- Current challenges: {challenges}

Produce five sections:

1. Cadence — frequency per person (monthly / quarterly / semi-annual), duration per session, cycle length. Sustainable given your total count.

2. Format — agenda structure (half their agenda, half yours is common). Who leads. Confidentiality norms: what stays private, what you'll bring up with the manager with or without attribution.

3. Topics — standing topics (team health, their career, what's blocking their manager's team) vs. rotating (strategic context, cross-team issues).

4. What to do with signal — how info flows back. When do you tell the manager immediately? When do you tell the manager without attribution? When do you sit on it?

5. Anti-patterns — things to avoid. Spying on managers. Solving problems that go around the manager. Becoming the de facto manager. Letting skip-levels become status updates.

Keep total under 600 words. Trust is the asset; signal is the byproduct.

prompts.name: Async vs sync rebalance
prompts.use_case: Rebalance the team's async-vs-sync mix when meetings have crowded out thinking time.
inputs: Current meeting load (hours per week per person)
Work types the team does (makers vs. managers, discovery vs. delivery)
Specific meetings/decisions that are currently sync
output_format: 'Load analysis' — current hours per role, thresholds of concern
'Sync-to-async candidates' — meetings that should become async (status updates, FYI, broad reviews)
'Async-to-sync candidates' — decisions or alignment that's currently async but suffers
'Async mechanisms' — written status, pre-reads, decision docs, Loom/video
'Sync preservation' — the sync time that should be protected (coaching, hard conversations, real-time collaboration)
'Transition plan' — how to shift the team without chaos
review_checklist: Load analysis uses real hours, not 'too many meetings'
Sync-to-async candidates consider the relationship signal the meeting provided
Sync preservation is honest (some things require real time)
Transition plan addresses the coordination cost of moving async
prompts.prompt:
You are rebalancing a team's async-vs-sync mix when meetings have crowded out thinking time.

Context
- Current meeting load per person: {load}
- Work types: {work_types}
- Currently-sync decisions/meetings: {sync_items}
- Company's cultural norm: {cultural_norm}

Produce six sections:

1. Load analysis — current hours per role. Thresholds of concern: makers > 15 hours in meetings have little building time; managers > 25 hours have little thinking time.

2. Sync-to-async candidates — meetings that should become async: status updates, FYI briefings, broad reviews. Consider: what relationship/visibility signal did the meeting provide? If non-zero, design the async replacement to preserve it.

3. Async-to-sync candidates — decisions currently async that suffer (e.g., hard debates that go in circles on Slack; feedback conversations that need real-time).

4. Async mechanisms — what replaces the cut meetings: written status, pre-reads + async comment, decision docs, Loom/video for nuance.

5. Sync preservation — the sync time that MUST be protected: coaching, hard conversations, real-time collaboration on ambiguous problems. Name it so it doesn't get cut in the same pass.

6. Transition plan — how to shift without chaos. Phase in async, validate signal quality, adjust.

Keep total under 700 words. Async-by-default is earned, not imposed.

prompts.title: Org Diagnosis Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for diagnosing org health: symptom-to-cause analysis, manager effectiveness signals, team health diagnostic, and span-of-control assessment.
prompts.why_it_matters: Most leadership problems are misdiagnosed: a performance issue that's actually a clarity issue, a team issue that's actually a manager issue, a reorg justified by symptoms that reveal a strategy problem. These prompts separate symptom from cause.
when_to_use: When a team is struggling and the root cause isn't clear
Evaluating manager effectiveness before reorg or promotion decisions
Diagnosing team health when survey or retention signals change
Assessing span-of-control sustainability as org grows
not_for: Using diagnosis to avoid the hard decision (when the diagnosis is clear, the call still has to be made)
Replacing 1:1 conversations with the affected people
source_inspiration: Patrick Lencioni — The Advantage, on org health
Amy Edmondson — The Fearless Organization, on psychological safety
Will Larson — An Elegant Puzzle on diagnosing dysfunction
Reorg (Heidari-Robinson & Heywood) on assessing need before restructuring

inputs_required.name: Presenting symptoms
inputs_required.description: The observable issue(s) that triggered the diagnosis.

inputs_required.name: Context
inputs_required.description: Team size, scope, history, recent changes.

inputs_required.name: Signal data
inputs_required.description: Surveys, attrition, performance, incident data as available.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Separate symptom from cause. Evidence from opinion.
prompts: A symptom is observable; a cause is inferred.
'The team is struggling' is a symptom, not a diagnosis.
Multiple causes can produce the same symptom; distinguish them.

prompts.name: Symptom-to-cause analysis
prompts.use_case: Given an observable symptom (missed commitments, attrition, low morale), diagnose the underlying cause.
inputs: Recent context (team changes, scope changes, leadership changes)
output_format: 'Symptom restated' — specific, observable, measured
'Candidate causes' — 4–6 plausible causes across categories (strategy clarity, team capability, manager capability, org structure, resourcing, external)
'Evidence for / against each cause' — what data supports, what contradicts
'Most likely cause' — best candidate with confidence level
'What would confirm the diagnosis' — specific evidence you'd look for
'Intervention per cause' — what to do if each candidate were the real cause
review_checklist: Symptom restated is observable, not characterizing
Candidate causes include at least one structural and one leadership cause
Evidence for/against is honest; not cherry-picked
Most likely cause confidence level is honest
Interventions differ meaningfully by cause (wrong-cause intervention is worse than no intervention)
prompts.prompt:
You are diagnosing an org symptom. Multiple causes can produce the same symptom; wrong-cause interventions make things worse.

Context
- Presenting symptom: {symptom}
- Recent context: {context}
- Available data: {data}

Produce six sections:

1. Symptom restated — specific, observable, measured. NOT 'team is struggling' but 'Team Y missed 2 of 4 commitments in Q2; 2 of 6 engineers rated the team <7 on the last survey; 1 attrition regrettable'.

2. Candidate causes — 4 to 6 across categories: strategy clarity, team capability, manager capability, org structure, resourcing, external (cross-team, market). At least one structural and one leadership cause.

3. Evidence for / against each cause — what data supports it, what contradicts. Honest.

4. Most likely cause — best candidate with confidence (high / medium / low). Don't pretend higher confidence than you have.

5. What would confirm the diagnosis — specific evidence you'd look for before committing to intervention (e.g., 'does the team's 1:1 feedback about manager X echo the survey data?').

6. Intervention per cause — what to do if each candidate were the real cause. This surfaces how different the right intervention is by cause.

Keep total under 800 words. Wrong diagnosis produces wrong intervention.

prompts.name: Manager effectiveness signals
prompts.use_case: Evaluate a manager's effectiveness before a reorg, promotion, or PIP decision — across people, delivery, and strategic dimensions.
output_format: 'People dimension' — 1:1 quality, feedback culture, growth of reports, retention
'Delivery dimension' — commitment reliability, quality, incident leadership
'Strategic dimension' — strategic thinking, prioritization, cross-team partnership
'Cultural dimension' — how the team talks about the manager, skip-level signal
'Overall read' — strong / developing / struggling, with confidence
'Development path or decision' — what to do with this read
review_checklist: Each dimension has observable signal (not 'seems engaged')
Team feedback is weighed, not quoted selectively
Overall read is calibrated to peer managers
Decision/path is specific (promote / coach / reassign / manage out)
prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating a manager's effectiveness for a decision (reorg, promotion, PIP). Use observable signal, not impression.

Context
- Manager: {manager_profile}
- Team outcomes: {team_outcomes}
- Team feedback: {team_feedback}
- Peer / manager-of-manager observations: {observer_notes}

Produce six sections:

1. People dimension — 1:1 quality (from skip-level signal), feedback culture, growth of reports (promotions, skill development), retention (regrettable attrition).

2. Delivery dimension — commitment reliability (what % of commitments landed), quality signal, incident leadership.

3. Strategic dimension — strategic thinking visible in planning and memos, prioritization quality, cross-team partnership.

4. Cultural dimension — how the team talks about the manager in skip-levels and retros. Weigh the balance, don't quote selectively.

5. Overall read — Strong / Developing / Struggling, with confidence level. Calibrate to peer managers at the same tier.

6. Development path or decision — what to do: promote, continue + coach, reassign to different scope, formal improvement plan, manage out. Specific recommendation.

Keep total under 700 words. Signal over impression.

prompts.name: Team health diagnostic
prompts.use_case: Diagnose team health across multiple dimensions — psychological safety, clarity, capability, workload, inclusion.
inputs: Survey data (eNPS, psych safety, manager score)
output_format: 'Psychological safety' — signal quality with evidence
'Clarity' — does the team know what they're working on, why, how success is measured?
'Capability' — do they have the skills and resources to succeed?
'Workload' — sustainable / stretched / burning?
'Inclusion' — does every member have voice and equitable experience?
'Overall health score' — with trend (improving / stable / deteriorating)
'Top 3 interventions'
review_checklist: Each dimension has observable signal, not 'feels good'
Psych safety is distinguished from agreement (healthy teams disagree well)
Workload assessment includes wake-ups, weekend work, PTO use
Interventions are specific to the dimension most in need
prompts.prompt:
You are diagnosing team health across multiple dimensions. Health is observable; don't settle for 'feels fine'.

Context
- Team profile: {team_profile}
- Survey data: {survey_data}
- Attrition/retention: {attrition_data}
- Observed dynamics: {observed_dynamics}

Produce seven sections:

1. Psychological safety — signal: does the team disagree openly? Does quiet dissent surface? Edmondson 7-item if you have it. Distinguish safety from agreement.

2. Clarity — does the team know what they're working on, why it matters, how success is measured? Signal: can each member articulate the current goal?

3. Capability — skills and resources to succeed. Signal: stretch assignments, skill gaps, tooling constraints.

4. Workload — sustainable / stretched / burning? Signal: wake-ups, weekend work, PTO use, burnout language in 1:1s.

5. Inclusion — does every member have voice and equitable experience? Signal: participation in meetings, promotion equity, feedback equity.

6. Overall health score — with trend (improving / stable / deteriorating).

7. Top 3 interventions — specific, targeted at the dimensions most in need. Not 'do team-building'; rather 'run an Edmondson audit next month' or 'rebalance on-call'.

Keep total under 800 words. Observable signal; honest assessment.

prompts.name: Span-of-control assessment
prompts.use_case: Assess whether current spans of control are sustainable given scope, tenure, and capability of each manager.
inputs: Org chart with spans per manager
Team scope and complexity per manager
Current pain signals (struggling managers, under-supported ICs)
output_format: 'Span table' — Manager | # of directs | Team scope | Tenure as manager | Complexity signal | Sustainability read
'Out-of-range spans' — managers with >8 directs (under-support) or <3 (over-management cost)
'Capability-adjusted read' — tenure-adjusted view (a new manager with 8 is different from a veteran with 8)
'Structural changes' — specific recommended adjustments (split spans, consolidate, promote sub-leads)
'Trade-offs' — what each adjustment costs (more managers = more overhead; larger spans = less support)
review_checklist: Complexity signal includes scope, cross-team dependencies, people issues
Capability-adjusted read is honest about tenure effects
Recommendations are specific (named manager, named change)
Trade-offs are acknowledged; there's no free lunch in span adjustment

prompts.title: Reorg Rollout Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for reorg execution: announcement drafter, manager 1:1 talking points, affected-IC FAQ, and post-reorg pulse-check synthesis.
prompts.why_it_matters: Reorgs succeed or fail at the rollout. A good design communicated poorly creates trust damage that outlasts the benefits. These prompts structure the comms and the post-check so the org can land the change.
when_to_use: Drafting the all-hands announcement after the reorg design is locked
Preparing managers for the cascade of 1:1s they need to run
Answering the predictable IC questions (comp, reporting, role, stability)
Synthesizing the first pulse-check 30–60 days after the reorg
not_for: Announcing a reorg without the 1:1 cascade first
Using comms to paper over a flawed design
source_inspiration: Heidari-Robinson & Heywood — ReOrg
Lara Hogan — reorg comms blog posts
Will Larson — reorg playbook for engineering orgs

inputs_required.name: Reorg design
inputs_required.description: Target topology, new reporting structure, what changes for whom.

inputs_required.name: Strategic rationale
inputs_required.description: Why this reorg, tied to outcomes.

inputs_required.name: Affected parties
inputs_required.description: Who's most affected (role change, reporting change, scope change).

inputs_required.name: Known concerns
inputs_required.description: Concerns already raised by managers or ICs.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: People should hear from their manager first, not in a broadcast.
Reasons tied to outcomes, not personalities.
Acknowledge disruption; don't pretend it's invisible.

prompts.name: Reorg announcement drafter
prompts.use_case: Draft the all-hands announcement delivered AFTER manager 1:1s have cascaded.
inputs: Tone (per company norm — warm, direct, executive)
output_format: 'Headline' — 2 sentences capturing the change
'Why now' — strategic rationale in 1 paragraph
'What's changing' — new topology / reporting at high level
'What's staying' — reassurance where reassurance is true (comp, benefits, seniority)
'What this means for you' — how each employee tier will see their world change
'Next steps' — timing, comms cadence, who to talk to
'Honest acknowledgment' — this is disruptive; what we're committing to do about it
review_checklist: Rationale ties to outcomes, not politics
What's staying includes specifics (don't promise comp stability if you can't)
Acknowledgment of disruption is real; not 'we're excited about this journey'
Next steps are specific with dates
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting the all-hands reorg announcement. This lands AFTER managers have run 1:1s with affected people.

Context
- Reorg design: {reorg_design}
- Strategic rationale: {rationale}
- What's changing vs. staying: {change_stay}
- Tone norm: {tone}

Produce seven sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences capturing what's changing and why.

2. Why now — strategic rationale in 1 paragraph. Tied to outcomes (customer, business, strategic bet), not to politics or personalities.

3. What's changing — new topology / reporting structure at high level. Not team-by-team in the all-hands; that happened in 1:1s.

4. What's staying — reassurance where it's TRUE: comp, benefits, team memberships that don't change, career trajectories. Don't promise what you can't hold.

5. What this means for you — how different groups experience the change: ICs with no reporting change, ICs with new manager, managers with new team, managers who are no longer managing.

6. Next steps — timing, comms cadence (when the next update is), who to talk to with concerns (specific people, not 'HR').

7. Honest acknowledgment — reorgs are disruptive. What we're committing to do about it (extra 1:1 time, open office hours, survey in 30 days). Real commitment.

Keep total under 800 words. Honest beats polished; reassurance where true.

prompts.name: Manager 1:1 talking points
prompts.use_case: Prepare managers for the cascade of 1:1s they need to run with their reports before the broader announcement.
inputs: Manager's team and who's affected how
The reorg design at the level relevant to this team
Likely reactions per report (based on manager's knowledge)
output_format: 'Opening language' — how to start the conversation so the person knows this is about them
'What's changing for them specifically' — per-report framing
'Their questions' — anticipated questions with answers
'What the manager does NOT yet know' — honest acknowledgment
'Confidentiality norms' — what can be shared, when
'What to do if they're upset' — the script for handling a hard reaction
review_checklist: Opening language is specific, doesn't bury the lede
Per-report framing addresses comp, reporting, scope, seniority explicitly
Manager-doesn't-yet-know section is honest
Hard-reaction script is real; some reactions will be hard
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing a manager to run the cascade of 1:1s with their team before the reorg announcement. The 1:1 is where the trust is won or lost.

Context
- Manager's team and how each is affected: {team_impact}
- Relevant reorg design: {design}
