- Likely reactions per report: {reactions}

Produce six sections:

1. Opening language — the first 30 seconds. Person needs to know this 1:1 is about them and the reorg, not regular business. Don't bury the lede.

2. What's changing for them specifically — per-report framing. Address the 4 things every engineer wants to know: comp, reporting line, scope, seniority.

3. Their questions — anticipated questions per report with answers. If the answer is 'I don't know yet,' say so.

4. What the manager does NOT yet know — honest acknowledgment of open questions. Trust is preserved by honesty about uncertainty.

5. Confidentiality norms — what the report can share, when. Typically: 'this will be announced broadly in 48 hours; until then, please hold'.

6. What to do if they're upset — the script for a hard reaction. Listen first. Don't debate. Acknowledge what's hard. Commit to follow-up.

Keep total under 700 words. Honest manager beats perfect script.

prompts.name: Affected-IC FAQ
prompts.use_case: Answer the predictable IC questions: comp, reporting, role, stability, timing.
output_format: 'FAQ' — 12–18 questions with honest answers, organized: comp | reporting | role | timing | process | support
'Questions we can't answer yet' — explicit with why and when we'll know
'How to raise concerns' — channels and expected response time
'What triggers us to revisit' — conditions under which we'd adjust the design
review_checklist: FAQ answers don't over-promise (especially on comp)
Can't-answer-yet section is real, not hiding
Raising-concerns channels are staffed and responsive
Revisit triggers are concrete

prompts.name: Post-reorg pulse-check synthesis
prompts.use_case: 30–60 days after the reorg, synthesize the pulse-check: what's working, what isn't, what needs adjustment.
output_format: 'Headline' — how the reorg is landing
'Working as intended' — evidence the design is delivering the intended outcomes
'Not yet working' — specific dysfunctions surfaced
'Attribution analysis' — which issues are reorg-caused vs. pre-existing
'Adjustments' — changes to make based on the pulse
'Next pulse cadence' — when we check again
review_checklist: Headline is honest (not 'everything's great' if it isn't)
Attribution analysis distinguishes reorg-caused from pre-existing
Adjustments are specific; don't wait for the next reorg to fix issues
Next pulse cadence is committed
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing the 30–60 day pulse-check after a reorg. Honest assessment; don't wait for the next reorg to adjust.

Context
- Pulse survey results: {survey}
- 1:1 themes from managers: {themes}
- Regrettable attrition since reorg: {attrition}
- Delivery signals: {delivery}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences on how the reorg is landing. Honest; include bad news.

2. Working as intended — evidence the design is delivering the intended outcomes. Specific: 'the new platform team has shipped two API contracts the stream-aligned teams requested'.

3. Not yet working — specific dysfunctions surfaced: unclear ownership, increased coordination cost, morale dip, attrition.

4. Attribution analysis — which issues are reorg-caused (new from the change) vs. pre-existing (were there before, now visible). Pre-existing issues don't justify rolling back the reorg.

5. Adjustments — changes to make based on the pulse. Specific: 'clarify ownership between Team A and Team B on X'; 'add skip-level cadence for new manager Y'. Don't wait for the next reorg cycle.

6. Next pulse cadence — when we check again (typically 60 days later, then quarterly).

Keep total under 700 words. Honest pulse beats theater.

prompts.title: Stakeholder Communication Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for cross-functional stakeholder comms: stakeholder map to tailored comms plan, difficult-news drafter, cross-functional status update, and exec status synthesis.
prompts.why_it_matters: Stakeholder comms either build credibility or spend it. The difference is whether the message is tailored to what the stakeholder actually cares about, delivered in a format they use, at a cadence they trust.
when_to_use: Building a tailored comms plan from a stakeholder map
Drafting difficult news (project slip, scope cut, miss)
Writing the recurring cross-functional status update
Synthesizing status for an exec audience
not_for: Generic updates broadcast to everyone regardless of interest
Hiding bad news behind softer framing
source_inspiration: Barbara Minto — The Pyramid Principle
Bryar & Carr — Working Backwards on PR/FAQ and 6-pagers
First Round Review — interviews on difficult-news delivery

inputs_required.name: Stakeholder map
inputs_required.description: Who cares about this work, at what level, with what concerns.

inputs_required.name: Current state
inputs_required.description: Where the work is, what's on track, what's at risk.

inputs_required.name: News to communicate
inputs_required.description: Specific update content.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Tailored beats broadcast. Honest beats polished.
prompts: Different stakeholders need different content, different format, different cadence.
Bad news delivered early lands better than polished news delivered late.
Acknowledge impact specifically.

prompts.name: Stakeholder map to tailored comms plan
prompts.use_case: Convert a stakeholder map into a tailored comms plan — for each stakeholder, what they care about, at what cadence, in what format.
inputs: Initiative or program the comms supports
output_format: 'Stakeholder matrix' — Stakeholder | Role | What they care about | Cadence | Format | Channel | Who drives
'Tier 1' (high power / high interest) — detailed plans per stakeholder
'Tier 2' (high power / low interest OR high interest / low power) — standard cadence
'Tier 3' (low power / low interest) — broadcast only
'Anti-patterns' — stakeholders over-communicated with and under-communicated with
review_checklist: Matrix captures what each stakeholder actually cares about, not what you want to tell them
Tier 1 plans are individualized, not templated
Cadence matches what the stakeholder can absorb
Anti-patterns identifies over-comm (credibility drain) and under-comm (surprise risk)
prompts.prompt:
You are converting a stakeholder map into a tailored comms plan. Tailored beats broadcast.

Context
- Stakeholder map: {stakeholder_map}
- Initiative: {initiative}
- Available channels: {channels}

Produce five sections:

1. Stakeholder matrix — table: Stakeholder | Role | What they care about (specifically) | Cadence | Format | Channel | Who drives the comm.

2. Tier 1 (high power / high interest) — detailed comms plan per stakeholder. Individualized. Examples of topics they'll care about, format they prefer.

3. Tier 2 (high power / low interest OR low power / high interest) — standard cadence comms; lighter touch.

4. Tier 3 (low power / low interest) — broadcast only (newsletter, all-hands callout).

5. Anti-patterns — stakeholders over-communicated with (credibility drain) and under-communicated with (surprise risk). Name them.

Keep total under 700 words. Match the comm to the stakeholder's actual interest.

prompts.name: Difficult-news drafter
prompts.use_case: Draft a message delivering hard news (project slip, scope cut, missed commitment) — honest, specific, with the next step.
output_format: 'Headline' — the bad news in 2 sentences, up front
'Impact on you' — per-audience specific impact
'What happened' — honest, factual, not making excuses
'What we're doing' — mitigation, corrective action
'What we commit to' — specific commitments with dates
'Your questions' — anticipated with honest answers
'How to raise concerns' — channels
review_checklist: Headline is up front; no slow-walking bad news
What happened is honest; acknowledges the call that led here
Commitments are real (not 'we'll do better')
Per-audience impact is specific, not generic

prompts.name: Cross-functional status update
prompts.use_case: Draft the recurring status update for cross-functional partners (Product, Design, Data, Sales).
inputs: Cross-functional partners and what they need to know
output_format: 'TL;DR' — 2–3 bullets covering what they need to know in 30 seconds
'Progress' — per work stream, what's shipped, what's in flight
'Risks' — surfaced honestly, with what we're doing
'Cross-functional asks' — specific asks of specific partners with dates
'What we're watching' — signals to revisit next update
review_checklist: TL;DR includes risks, not just progress
Cross-functional asks are specific and dated
Risks section doesn't hide what's at risk
Format is consistent across updates (partners develop scan patterns)

prompts.name: Exec status synthesis
prompts.use_case: Synthesize detailed team status into the 1-paragraph version an exec actually reads.
inputs: Detailed status across the org (team lead updates, program reports)
Exec audience and what they care about
Top 3 decisions we want them aware of or asking about
output_format: 'Exec paragraph' — 3–5 sentences: the state, the one big thing, the one risk, the ask if any
'Supporting bullets' — 3–5 bullets with more detail for those who want it
'Metric callouts' — 2–3 numbers that anchor the narrative
'Questions we'd welcome' — questions the exec might ask that we'd want the opportunity to answer
review_checklist: Exec paragraph is tight (3–5 sentences, no more)
One big thing and one risk are honestly the most important
Metrics anchor the narrative, don't decorate it
Questions-we'd-welcome section positions for good dialogue
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing detailed team status into a version an exec will read in 60 seconds.

Context
- Detailed status: {detailed_status}
- Exec audience interests: {exec_interests}
- Top 3 decisions to surface: {decisions_to_surface}

Produce four sections:

1. Exec paragraph — 3 to 5 sentences. The state of the work, the ONE big thing (not five), the ONE risk worth their attention, the ask if any. Tight.

2. Supporting bullets — 3 to 5 bullets with more detail for execs who want to go one level deeper.

3. Metric callouts — 2 to 3 numbers that anchor the narrative. Not decoration; evidence.

4. Questions we'd welcome — questions the exec might ask that we'd want the chance to answer. Positions for good dialogue rather than defensiveness.

Keep total under 400 words. Compression is the value-add.

prompts.title: Board Narrative Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for board-level narrative: board memo drafter, wins/risks/asks structure, anticipated board questions, and the multi-quarter story arc.
prompts.why_it_matters: Board members read 200 pages a week. Your section either stands out for clarity and judgment or blurs into the deck. These prompts force the discipline.
when_to_use: Drafting the engineering section of the board pre-read
Structuring wins/risks/asks for the board meeting itself
Preparing for board questions that go past the deck
Crafting the multi-quarter story arc that shows engineering's strategic trajectory
not_for: Boards under NDA hearing privileged information you can't share — check with CEO/CFO/legal
Replacing CEO/CFO judgment on board framing
source_inspiration: Bryar & Carr — Working Backwards on PR/FAQ format
Ben Horowitz — The Hard Thing About Hard Things on board comms
First Round Review — board meeting interviews

inputs_required.name: Quarter context
inputs_required.description: OKRs, major milestones, customer events.

inputs_required.name: Board context
inputs_required.description: Who's on the board, what they care about, prior concerns raised.

inputs_required.name: Quantitative results
inputs_required.description: Engineering metrics, customer impact, financial.

inputs_required.name: CEO/CFO framing
inputs_required.description: How the rest of the deck is framing the quarter.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Clarity over completeness; honest about risks.
prompts: Board members reward judgment, not data dumps.
Risks called out early build credibility.
Asks are specific and consequential.

prompts.name: Board memo drafter
prompts.use_case: Draft the engineering memo for the board pre-read — 2 pages, narrative-led, with the deck as supporting detail.
inputs: How CEO/CFO is framing the quarter
output_format: 'Headline' — 2 sentences capturing the quarter at engineering altitude
'Strategic context' — 1 paragraph on how engineering's work connects to company strategy
'Wins' — 3 wins, each with concrete outcome and what it enables
'Risks' — 2–3 risks with what we're doing
'Capability investments' — what we built (people, platform) for the future
'Asks of the board' — specific (decisions, sponsorship, intros)
review_checklist: Memo is 2 pages, not 6
Wins are outcomes, not activities
Risks are surfaced honestly
Asks are real (not 'continued support')
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting the engineering memo for the board pre-read. Two pages. Narrative-led. Concrete.

Context
- Quarter: {quarter_context}
- Board: {board_context}
- Quantitative results: {quant_results}
- CEO/CFO framing: {ceo_cfo_framing}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences capturing the quarter at engineering altitude. Strategic, not operational.

2. Strategic context — 1 paragraph on how engineering's work connects to the company strategy this quarter.

3. Wins — 3 wins. Each: concrete outcome (numbers, not adjectives) and what this enables. NOT 'shipped 12 features' but 'launched checkout v2 driving 8% conversion uplift in EU, unblocking the 2026 expansion plan'.

4. Risks — 2 to 3 risks. What's at risk, what we're doing, when we'll know. Don't hide.

5. Capability investments — what we built (people, platform) for the future. Not flashy, but matters strategically.

6. Asks of the board — specific. A decision they need to make, sponsorship for a hire, an introduction. NOT 'continued support'.

Keep total under 800 words. Two pages of substance.

prompts.name: Wins / risks / asks structure
prompts.use_case: Convert quarter inputs into the board-meeting-ready wins/risks/asks structure (often the live verbal section in the meeting).
inputs: Risks the engineering team is carrying
Asks the board can act on
output_format: 'Top 3 wins' — each with: outcome, evidence, strategic significance
'Top 3 risks' — each with: what's at risk, current mitigation, request for board (if any)
'Top 2 asks' — specific, board-actionable
'Time allocation' — minutes per section to fit the slot
'What we'd say if asked for one wish' — the most important thing the board could do for engineering
review_checklist: Wins are 3, not 7 (board meetings reward judgment about what's important)
Risks include the one you'd rather not bring up — that's the one to lead with
Asks are board-actionable (not 'help us recruit')
One-wish forces the prioritization

prompts.name: Anticipated board questions
prompts.use_case: Prepare for board questions that go past the deck — competitive, technical-strategic, talent, and 'what aren't you telling us'.
inputs: Board's recent themes (acquisitions, competition, AI, growth)
Areas the board has previously pushed on
output_format: 'Top 5 likely questions' — each with the question and your honest answer
'Top 3 hardest questions' — ones you don't have a clean answer to
'Questions about competitors' — anticipated competitive questions with framing
'Talent questions' — board often asks about top performers, gaps, attrition
'What aren't you telling us' — questions a sharp board member might ask to test whether you're hiding something
review_checklist: Hardest questions are real (you actually don't have answers; not theater)
Competitive answers don't dismiss competitors
Talent answers include named individuals if appropriate (board often wants to know about key talent)
'What aren't you telling us' has a real answer
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing for board questions that go past the deck. The board questions test judgment.

Context
- Memo and verbal structure: {memo_verbal}
- Board's recent themes: {recent_themes}
- Areas board has pushed on before: {prior_pushes}

Produce five sections:

1. Top 5 likely questions — each: the question (in board voice if possible), your honest answer.

2. Top 3 hardest questions — ones you don't have a clean answer to. For each: the question, what you'll say honestly, the follow-up plan.

3. Questions about competitors — anticipated competitive questions. Framing that's confident without dismissing competitors (boards see through dismissal).

4. Talent questions — board often asks about top performers, key gaps, attrition. Be ready with names (with appropriate confidentiality framing).

5. 'What aren't you telling us' — the question a sharp board member asks. Have a real answer; saying 'nothing' loses credibility instantly. Examples: a brewing risk you've decided to manage rather than escalate; a hire that didn't work out; a project quietly killed.

Keep total under 700 words. Boards reward candor; punish polish.

prompts.name: Multi-quarter story arc
prompts.use_case: Frame the current quarter in the context of the multi-quarter strategic arc — where engineering is going, what bets are paying off, what's next.
output_format: 'Where we said we'd go' — the strategic arc as articulated 2–4 quarters ago
'Where we are' — honest assessment of progress on that arc
'What's working' — bets that are paying off, with evidence
'What's not working' — bets that aren't, with what we're doing
'Where we're going next' — the next strategic chapter, with rationale
'How board can help' — specific, ongoing
review_checklist: Where-we-said-we'd-go is honest; not retconning
What's-not-working is real (every multi-quarter arc has something that didn't pan out)
Where-we're-going-next builds on what we learned
Help-from-board is specific and ongoing, not 'support'
prompts.prompt:
You are framing this quarter's engineering story in the multi-quarter strategic arc. Boards trust narratives that have continuity and honesty about pivots.

Context
- This quarter's narrative: {this_quarter}
- Last 2–4 quarters of board updates: {prior_quarters}
- 12–24 month strategic direction: {strategic_direction}

Produce six sections:

1. Where we said we'd go — the strategic arc articulated 2 to 4 quarters ago. Honest reading; don't retcon.

2. Where we are — honest assessment of progress on that arc. What we've done, what we haven't, what's changed.

3. What's working — bets paying off with concrete evidence. The arc's wins.

4. What's not working — bets that aren't, with what we're doing (kill, pivot, double down). Every multi-quarter arc has something that didn't pan out; pretending otherwise loses credibility.

5. Where we're going next — the next strategic chapter. Should build on what worked and respond to what didn't.

6. How board can help — specific, ongoing requests (intros, sponsorship, governance, decisions).

Keep total under 800 words. Continuity beats reinvention.

prompts.title: Budget Planning Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for budget planning: annual budget narrative, variance analysis, headcount-vs-cost-vs-output framing, and cost-of-status-quo (what cuts mean).
prompts.why_it_matters: Engineering budget conversations either land on outcomes or get reduced to per-engineer cost. The narrative is the bridge — connecting the team's spend to the company's bets and to the cost of NOT investing.
when_to_use: Drafting the annual engineering budget narrative
Analyzing variance against plan (over or under)
Framing the headcount-vs-cost-vs-output trade-off for non-engineering execs
Articulating the cost of status quo when budget cuts are proposed
not_for: Bypassing the CFO/finance partner — budget decisions are partnered, not authored alone
Hiding overspend or unfunded commitments
source_inspiration: Mark Suster — finance reading for tech operators
FinOps Foundation — cloud cost frameworks
First Round Review — interviews with engineering CFOs

inputs_required.name: Current state
inputs_required.description: Current budget, headcount, major spend categories.

inputs_required.name: Strategic objectives
inputs_required.description: What the company is trying to do, what engineering needs to invest in.

inputs_required.name: Constraints
inputs_required.description: Funding pressure, growth/efficiency mode, board context.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Connect spend to outcomes the business cares about.
Variance is causal — name what drove it.
The cost of NOT spending is real; quantify it.

prompts.name: Annual budget narrative
prompts.use_case: Draft the annual engineering budget narrative for the CEO/CFO/board — what we're investing in, why, what it produces.
inputs: Current spend by category (HC, cloud, tools, vendors)
output_format: 'Headline' — 2 sentences capturing the budget posture
'Spend by category' — table with prior year, current year, % change
'Strategic logic' — for each major change, the rationale tied to outcomes
'What we're cutting' — explicit reductions
'What we're committing to deliver' — outputs/outcomes the budget enables
'Sensitivity' — what happens if budget is reduced by 10% or 20%
review_checklist: Spend table is honest (not hiding contractor spend or vendor renewals)
Strategic logic ties to outcomes the business cares about
Cuts are real (not 'efficiency')
Sensitivity analysis is honest about what gets dropped
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting the annual engineering budget narrative for the CEO/CFO/board.

Context
- Current spend by category: {current_spend}
- Proposed change vs. last year: {proposed_change}
- Strategic objectives requiring investment: {strategic_objectives}
- Outputs the budget enables: {outputs}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — 2 sentences capturing budget posture (growth, efficient growth, efficiency, retrenchment).

2. Spend by category — table: Category | Prior year | Current year | % change. Categories: HC (FT + contractor), cloud, tools/SaaS, vendors, T&E. Be honest; include contractor and vendor renewals.

3. Strategic logic — for each major change (>10% category move), the rationale tied to outcomes the business cares about (not engineering KPIs).

4. What we're cutting — explicit reductions, by category. Not 'efficiency'. Real cuts.

5. What we're committing to deliver — outputs/outcomes the budget enables. Tie to OKRs and strategic objectives.

6. Sensitivity — what happens if budget is reduced 10% or 20%. Honest: what gets dropped, what slows down. Not 'we'd be more efficient'.

Keep total under 800 words. Connect spend to outcomes.

prompts.name: Budget variance analysis
prompts.use_case: Analyze actual spend vs. plan — what drove the variance, what to do about it.
output_format: 'Headline' — net variance (over / under, by what %)
'Variance by category' — table with plan, actual, variance, cause
'Causal analysis' — what drove the variance (one-time vs. trend, controllable vs. external)
'Top 3 surprises' — variances we didn't anticipate
'Forecast revision' — updated outlook for the rest of the period
'Recommended actions' — accept, adjust forecast, take corrective action
review_checklist: Causes are causal, not narrative ('cloud overspend caused by Y migration' not 'increased usage')
One-time vs. trend distinction is honest
Forecast revision is real (not 'we'll catch up')
Actions are specific (not 'monitor closely')
prompts.prompt:
You are analyzing budget variance against plan. Causal, honest, action-oriented.

Context
- Plan vs actual: {plan_actual}
- Period: {period}
- Major events: {events}

Produce six sections:

1. Headline — net variance: over by X% / under by Y% / on plan.

2. Variance by category — table: Category | Plan | Actual | Variance ($ and %) | Cause.

3. Causal analysis — what drove the variance. Distinguish one-time (a single migration) from trend (sustained higher usage). Distinguish controllable (vendor we picked) from external (price changes).

4. Top 3 surprises — variances we didn't anticipate. These are the highest-leverage learnings for next planning.

5. Forecast revision — updated outlook for the rest of the period. Honest; don't assume self-correction.

6. Recommended actions — for each material variance: accept (and adjust forecast), adjust forecast (no action needed), take corrective action (specific). Not 'monitor closely'.

Keep total under 600 words. Causal explanation; specific actions.

prompts.name: Headcount-vs-cost-vs-output framing
prompts.use_case: Frame the relationship between headcount, cost, and output for non-engineering executives — addressing 'why does engineering cost so much?' directly.
inputs: Comparable benchmarks (industry, peer companies if available)
output_format: 'Cost composition' — breakdown showing where the money goes (HC vs infra vs vendors)
'Output per dollar' — how much output the spend produces, with anchors
'Why HC is the major cost' — explanation for non-engineering audience
'Where the leverage is' — investments that change output-per-dollar
'Apples-to-apples comparisons' — relevant benchmarks with caveats
review_checklist: Cost composition is honest (HC is typically 70%+ of eng cost)
Output measures are real (not vanity)
Why-HC explanation lands for non-eng audience
Comparisons acknowledge caveats (different scopes, different missions)

prompts.name: Cost of status quo (what cuts mean)
prompts.use_case: When budget cuts are proposed, articulate the cost of the cut — what slows, what stops, what risks compound.
inputs: Cuts we'd make at increasing severity (5%, 10%, 20%)
output_format: 'Cut summary' — what's being proposed
'What slows' — outputs that take longer; quantify the slowdown
'What stops' — outputs that won't happen at all; what we lose
'What risks compound' — risks (reliability, security, attrition) that worsen without funding
'Customer/revenue impact' — downstream business effects
'Alternatives' — different cut shapes that achieve the same dollar savings with less damage
'Recommendation' — what we'd accept vs. what we'd contest
review_checklist: Slowdown is quantified, not vague
What stops is honest (don't claim everything continues at lower budget)
Compounding risks are specific (not 'morale')
Alternatives are real, not strawmen
prompts.prompt:
You are articulating the cost of a proposed budget cut. Honest about what we'd lose; constructive about alternatives.

Context
- Proposed cut: {proposed_cut}
- Current commitments and roadmap: {commitments}
- Severity scenarios (5/10/20% cuts): {severity_scenarios}

Produce seven sections:

1. Cut summary — what's being proposed (amount, category, timing).

2. What slows — outputs that take longer at the new budget. Quantify: 'feature X moves from Q3 to Q1 next year'.

3. What stops — outputs that won't happen at all. Honest. Don't claim everything continues.

4. What risks compound — risks that worsen without funding. Reliability (deferred SLO investment leads to incidents), security (deferred patches), attrition (key hires lost), capability (platform investment frozen).

5. Customer/revenue impact — downstream business effects. Specific customer commitments at risk, revenue contracts dependent on roadmap items.

6. Alternatives — different cut shapes that achieve the same dollar savings with less damage. Not 'don't cut' but 'cut Y instead of X' or 'phase the cut over 2 quarters'.

7. Recommendation — what we'd accept vs. what we'd contest. Pick your battles; don't fight every cut.

Keep total under 700 words. Honest cost; constructive alternatives.

prompts.title: Business Ops Digest Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for the operating digest: weekly leadership digest, cross-team status synthesis, anomaly callout, and action-item rollup.
prompts.why_it_matters: The weekly digest either gets read or it doesn't. Most don't. The difference is signal density: anomalies highlighted, decisions surfaced, action items owned. Status updates that read like changelog summaries are theater.
when_to_use: Drafting the recurring weekly leadership digest
Synthesizing cross-team status from multiple inputs
Highlighting anomalies that warrant leadership attention
Rolling up action items across the org with status
not_for: Replacing the in-person staff meeting (digest complements, not replaces)
Burying bad news in the middle of a long status update
source_inspiration: Amazon — written digest culture
Stripe — internal newsletter format
GitLab Handbook — async status reporting

inputs_required.name: Reporting period
inputs_required.description: Typically last 7 days.

inputs_required.name: Team inputs
inputs_required.description: Status updates from each team or area.

inputs_required.name: Audience
inputs_required.description: Who reads this digest, what they need to scan for.

inputs_required.name: Open action items
inputs_required.description: From prior digests, with status.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: Anomalies up top.
Decisions named, not narrated.
Action items have owners and dates.

prompts.name: Weekly leadership digest drafter
prompts.use_case: Draft the weekly leadership digest from team inputs — scannable, signal-dense, decision-oriented.
inputs: Audience and what they scan for
output_format: 'TL;DR' — 3 bullets covering the week's signal
'Anomalies / things to know' — at top, where attention belongs
'By area' — short status per major area (3–5 lines each)
'Decisions and changes' — what was decided this week
'Watch list' — things we're tracking that could move
'Coming up' — what's on the calendar that affects the audience
review_checklist: TL;DR includes the bad signal, not just progress
Anomalies are at top, not buried
Per-area status is brief (5 lines max)
Decisions are named, not narrated
prompts.prompt:
You are drafting the weekly leadership digest. Audience: {audience}. They scan in 3 minutes.

Context
- Team inputs: {team_inputs}
- Notable events this week: {events}

Produce six sections:

1. TL;DR — 3 bullets covering this week's signal. Include bad signal, not just progress.

2. Anomalies / things to know — at top. What's unusual this week that the audience needs to know.

3. By area — short status per major area. 3 to 5 lines max each. Outcome language; not activity log.

4. Decisions and changes — what was decided this week. Named (decision, decider, date).

5. Watch list — things we're tracking that could move. Brief (incident exposure, capacity tension, vendor situation).

6. Coming up — what's on the calendar that affects this audience next week.

Keep total under 600 words. Signal density beats completeness.

prompts.name: Cross-team status synthesis
prompts.use_case: Synthesize status from multiple teams into a coherent leadership-altitude view.
inputs: Strategic priorities to anchor the synthesis
What the audience is most concerned about
output_format: 'Strategic-priority view' — for each top priority, what's progressing across teams
'Cross-team risks' — risks that span teams (dependency, contention, capability)
'Cross-team wins' — wins enabled by collaboration across teams
'Surprising patterns' — themes you didn't expect when reading individual updates
'What needs leadership attention' — items requiring action one altitude up
review_checklist: Strategic-priority view shows progress, not activity
Cross-team risks include dependencies and capability gaps
Surprising patterns is real (what did you learn from reading them all together)
Leadership-attention items are specific asks
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing cross-team status from multiple inputs into a leadership view. The synthesis is the value-add; status copying is not.

Context
- Raw team inputs: {team_inputs}
- Strategic priorities: {priorities}
- Audience concerns: {audience_concerns}

Produce five sections:

1. Strategic-priority view — for each top priority, what's progressing across teams. Cross-team perspective; not 'team A says X, team B says Y'.

2. Cross-team risks — risks that span teams: dependency chains at risk, capacity contention, capability gaps that no single team can solve.

3. Cross-team wins — wins enabled by collaboration across teams. Worth highlighting because cross-team coordination is hard and worth recognizing.

4. Surprising patterns — themes that emerge when you read all the updates together that no single team named. Often the highest-signal section.

5. What needs leadership attention — items requiring action one altitude up. Specific asks, not 'continued visibility'.

Keep total under 600 words. Synthesis over copy-paste.

prompts.name: Anomaly callout
prompts.use_case: Surface anomalies from the week's data and inputs that warrant leadership attention — and frame them.
inputs: Metric data for the week (deliverability, reliability, throughput, attrition)
Comparison baseline (last week, last month, plan)
output_format: 'Anomaly inventory' — list of unusual items detected
'Top 3 anomalies by significance' — ranked by what would change if leadership knew
'For each top anomaly' — what's anomalous, what we know about cause, what action (if any) is recommended
'False alarms' — anomalies that look concerning but aren't
'What's normal that should worry us' — patterns that are 'normal' but reflect underlying drift
review_checklist: Anomaly significance is honest (not every fluctuation is significant)
Cause analysis distinguishes confirmed from suspected
False alarms are explicit (suppresses noise)
'Normal but worrying' is the underrated section — chronic drift hides in normal
prompts.prompt:
You are surfacing anomalies that warrant leadership attention from the week's data and signals.

Context
- Metric data: {metrics}
- Team-level signals: {signals}
- Comparison baseline: {baseline}

Produce five sections:

1. Anomaly inventory — list of unusual items detected.

2. Top 3 anomalies by significance — ranked by what would change if leadership knew.

3. For each top anomaly — what's anomalous (specific change vs baseline), what we know about cause (confirmed vs suspected), what action (if any) is recommended.

4. False alarms — anomalies that look concerning but aren't (one-time event, expected variation, instrumentation noise). Explicit; suppresses noise for next time.

5. What's normal that should worry us — patterns that are 'normal' but reflect underlying drift (slowly increasing on-call hours, slowly decreasing eNPS, slow incident-rate increase). Underrated section; chronic problems hide here.

Keep total under 500 words. Honest anomalies; explicit false alarms.

prompts.name: Action item rollup
prompts.use_case: Roll up open action items across the org with status, owners, and what's slipping.
inputs: All open action items (from prior digests, retros, postmortems)
output_format: 'Status summary' — total open, on track, at risk, slipped
'On-track items' — brief; just confirm they're moving
'At-risk items' — items where the owner is signaling slippage; with the ask
'Slipped items' — past due; with reason and revised date or kill recommendation
'Aging concerns' — items open >90 days; should they be killed or escalated
'Newly added this week'
review_checklist: Status counts add up
At-risk items have a specific ask, not just 'monitoring'
Slipped items get a revised date or a kill recommendation, not perpetual extension
Aging items are challenged; long-open items should die or be promoted
prompts.prompt:
You are rolling up open action items across the org. Items either move, get re-committed, or die.

Context
- Open action items: {open_items}
- Status updates per item: {status_updates}
- Aging info: {aging}

Produce six sections:

1. Status summary — total open, on track, at risk, slipped, killed this period.

2. On-track items — brief; just confirm progress.

3. At-risk items — items where the owner is signaling slippage. With the specific ask (resource, decision, escalation).

4. Slipped items — past due. For each: reason, revised date OR kill recommendation. Don't extend perpetually.

5. Aging concerns — items open >90 days. Should they be killed (no longer relevant) or escalated (needs intervention)? Don't let them rot.

6. Newly added this week — items added to the rollup with owner and due date.

Keep total under 500 words. Items move or die.

prompts.title: Headcount Case Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for headcount business cases: case writer, ROI / cost-of-not-hiring framing, alternative-options stress-test, and approval objection responder.
prompts.why_it_matters: Headcount is the most expensive recurring decision a leader makes. Most cases lose because they advocate the role rather than analyze the trade-off. These prompts force the analysis.
when_to_use: Writing a headcount case for approval
Framing the cost of NOT hiring (often more important than the cost of hiring)
Stress-testing alternatives (do nothing, contractor, redeploy existing)
Preparing for the objections that always come
not_for: Adding HC without considering structural alternatives
Hiding ongoing cost (HC is recurring; one-time alternatives exist)
source_inspiration: Patty McCord — Powerful, on talent budget discipline
Standard FP&A case formats
Will Larson on engineering org math

inputs_required.name: Role and level
inputs_required.description: Specific role, level, geography.

inputs_required.name: Current capability gap
inputs_required.description: What can't be done today, by whom.

inputs_required.name: Outcomes the hire enables
inputs_required.description: Specific outputs or strategic capabilities.

inputs_required.name: Cost (fully loaded)
inputs_required.description: Salary + benefits + equity + tooling + space.

inputs_required.name: Alternatives considered
inputs_required.description: Don't hire, contractor, redeploy, training.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Analyze the trade-off, don't advocate the hire.
prompts: Capability gap is specific.
Cost of NOT hiring is real.
Alternatives are honestly considered.

prompts.name: Headcount business case writer
prompts.use_case: Draft the headcount business case — capability gap, outcomes, cost, alternatives, recommendation.
output_format: 'Headline' — the role and the case in 2 sentences
'Capability gap' — specific (not 'we need more engineers')
'Outcomes the hire enables' — concrete, with timing
'Cost (fully loaded)' — annual cost, 3-year projection
'Alternatives considered' — table with each alternative, cost, and why rejected
'Risk if we don't hire' — what happens by quarter if this stays unfunded
'Recommendation and ask'
review_checklist: Capability gap is specific (named work that won't get done, named decisions that won't get made)
Outcomes have timing (when does the hire start contributing)
Fully-loaded cost includes everything (typically 1.4–1.6x base salary)
Alternatives are real (including 'don't hire')

prompts.name: ROI / cost-of-not-hiring framing
prompts.use_case: Frame the ROI conversation: not just what the hire produces, but what the absence costs.
inputs: Hire's expected output (engineering capacity, leadership, capability)
Cost of NOT having this role (current quarter and compounding)
Quantitative anchors where available (revenue, customer impact, attrition risk)
output_format: 'Output projection' — what the hire produces by quarter (Q1: ramping, Q2: contributing, Q3+: full capacity)
'Cost of not hiring' — what's already breaking; what gets worse
'Quantitative ROI' — where you can quantify (sales-team-blocked-on-X = $Y revenue)
'Qualitative ROI' — capability you're building that compounds
'Break-even analysis' — when does the hire pay for themselves
review_checklist: Output projection accounts for ramp time honestly
Cost-of-not-hiring is concrete, not 'team morale will suffer'
Quantitative ROI is specific where possible
Break-even is realistic, not aggressive
prompts.prompt:
You are framing the ROI of a headcount ask. The case usually lives or dies in the cost-of-not-hiring section.

Context
- Hire's expected output: {expected_output}
- Cost of NOT having this role: {cost_of_absence}
- Quantitative anchors: {anchors}

Produce five sections:

1. Output projection — what the hire produces by quarter. Honest about ramp time. Q1 typically near-zero; full capacity Q3+.

2. Cost of not hiring — what's already breaking; what gets worse with continued absence. Concrete: 'three customer-promised features delayed by 1 quarter without this hire'; not 'team morale will suffer'.

3. Quantitative ROI — where you can quantify. Examples: sales team blocked on X = $Y revenue impact; capacity to take on customer Z = $W contract.

4. Qualitative ROI — capability you're building that compounds. Less measurable but real (technical leadership, mentorship, judgment in a domain).

5. Break-even analysis — when the hire pays for themselves in measurable output. Realistic.

Keep total under 500 words. Cost of absence is the hidden value.

prompts.name: Alternative-options stress-test
prompts.use_case: Stress-test the alternatives — make sure the case isn't 'hire vs. nothing' but a real consideration of options.
inputs: Realistic alternatives (don't hire, contractor for N months, redeploy, automate, defer)
output_format: 'Alternative table' — Option | Cost | Speed | Capability fit | Sustainability | Trade-offs
'Don't hire' — what we'd do instead and what we'd lose
'Contractor (N months)' — when bridging makes sense, when it doesn't
'Redeploy existing' — moving an engineer from area X to area Y; what we lose in X
'Automate / outsource' — when this is a real option vs. wishful
'Recommendation' — and why the alternatives don't dominate
review_checklist: Alternatives include 'don't hire' (often the right alternative)
Contractor option includes the conversion conversation
Redeploy is honest about what gets dropped in the source area
Recommendation defends against alternatives, doesn't dismiss them
prompts.prompt:
You are stress-testing alternatives to a headcount hire. The discipline: 'don't hire' is always an option.

Context
- Proposed hire: {proposed_hire}
- Realistic alternatives: {alternatives}
- Cost and trade-offs of each: {cost_tradeoffs}

Produce six sections:

1. Alternative table — Option | Cost | Speed | Capability fit | Sustainability | Trade-offs.

2. Don't hire — what we'd do instead and what we'd lose. Don't dismiss; this is often the right alternative.

3. Contractor (N months) — when bridging with a contractor makes sense (urgent, temporary scope, validation). When it doesn't (institutional knowledge, long-term ownership, leadership). Include the conversion conversation if applicable.

4. Redeploy existing — moving an engineer from area X to area Y. Honest about what gets dropped in the source area; that's the real cost.

5. Automate / outsource — when this is a real option vs. wishful. Most 'we'll automate this' plans don't ship.

6. Recommendation — and why the alternatives don't dominate. Defend against, don't dismiss.

Keep total under 600 words. Alternatives considered honestly is the credibility of the case.

prompts.name: Approval objection responder
prompts.use_case: Prepare for the objections from CFO/CEO/board that always come on headcount asks.
inputs: Likely objections (budget pressure, hiring freeze context, peer requests competing)
output_format: 'Top 5 likely objections' — each with: the objection, your honest response
'The hardest objection' — the one you don't have a clean answer to, with how you'd handle
'What you'd accept as a counter' — partial wins (lower level, contractor first, deferred start)
'What you'd not accept' — where the case dies if compromised (e.g., wrong level for the work)
'How to escalate if denied' — when to push, when to absorb the no
review_checklist: Hardest objection is real (where the case is genuinely weak)
Counter-acceptance is calibrated to what the role actually needs
Won't-accept items are real (don't pretend everything is negotiable)
Escalation guidance is honest about when to fight
prompts.prompt:
You are preparing for objections to a headcount case. Anticipate; don't get caught off-guard.

Context
- Headcount case: {case}
- Likely objections: {likely_objections}
- Approver's known concerns: {approver_concerns}

Produce five sections:

1. Top 5 likely objections — each with the objection (in approver voice if possible) and your honest response. Common objections: 'why now', 'why this level', 'why not contractor', 'budget pressure', 'other team has same ask'.

2. The hardest objection — the one you don't have a clean answer to. How you'd handle (acknowledge the gap, propose a check-in, accept as risk).

3. What you'd accept as a counter — partial wins. Lower level (with rationale), contractor for first 6 months, deferred start, geography flex.

4. What you'd NOT accept — where the case dies if compromised. Examples: 'wrong level for the work means we'd be back in 6 months'; 'contractor wouldn't get the institutional knowledge needed'.

5. How to escalate if denied — when to push (case is solid, denial is political/budget-cycle); when to absorb (case is weak, timing is wrong, broader context). Honest about when to fight.

Keep total under 600 words. Anticipated objections; honest responses.

prompts.estimated_time: 60–120 minutes for full case across all 4 prompts

prompts.title: ROI Narrative Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for ROI business cases: cost-of-status-quo analyzer, return quantifier, qualitative-return narrator, and approval-objection responder.
prompts.why_it_matters: ROI cases for engineering investment lose because they're written by engineers as engineering documents. These prompts force the financial framing — quantify what you can, narrate what you can't, surface the cost of NOT doing it, prepare for objections.
when_to_use: Building the cost-of-status-quo section of an investment case
Quantifying returns when the investment doesn't have direct revenue impact
Narrating qualitative returns (capability, optionality, defensibility)
Anticipating CFO/CEO objections to a substantial investment ask
not_for: Replacing the investment-roi-business-case template — these support sections of the case
Bypassing CFO/finance-partner cost validation
source_inspiration: FinOps Foundation — TCO patterns
Mark Suster — finance for tech operators
Investment-banking-style ROI structures adapted for engineering

inputs_required.name: Investment proposal
inputs_required.description: What we're proposing, scope, cost.

inputs_required.name: Status quo data
inputs_required.description: What we currently spend, what currently breaks, what we currently miss.

inputs_required.name: Audience
inputs_required.description: CFO, CEO, board — what each cares about.

sections.name: Usage guidance
sections.purpose: Quantify what you can; narrate what you can't.
prompts: Status quo is not zero cost; quantify it.
Connect to outcomes the audience cares about.
Honest about uncertainty in projections.

prompts.name: Cost-of-status-quo analyzer
prompts.use_case: Quantify the cost of NOT making the proposed investment. Often the strongest section of an ROI case.
inputs: What we currently spend in workarounds
What we miss (lost deals, customers, capabilities)
Compounding factors (debt accumulating, capability gap widening)
output_format: 'Direct workaround cost' — engineering time, vendor cost, customer support cost
'Cost of what breaks' — incidents, customer churn, SLA penalties
'Cost of what we miss' — deals lost, customers can't be served, capabilities competitors have
'Compounding analysis' — what gets worse over 12, 24, 36 months
'Risk costs' — risks that increase if the investment isn't made (security, compliance, reliability)
'3-year status-quo total' — running cost of doing nothing
review_checklist: Workaround cost includes engineer time at fully-loaded cost
Compounding analysis is realistic (debt and capability gaps grow non-linearly)
Risk costs are quantified (probability × impact)
Total presents status quo as expensive, because it usually is
prompts.prompt:
You are quantifying the cost of NOT making a proposed engineering investment. Status quo is not zero cost.

Context
- What we currently spend in workarounds: {workaround_spend}
- What currently breaks: {breaks}
- What we miss: {missed}
- Compounding factors: {compounding}

Produce six sections:

1. Direct workaround cost — engineer time spent maintaining workarounds (× fully-loaded cost), vendor cost for stop-gaps, customer support cost. Quantified annually.

2. Cost of what breaks — incidents tied to this gap (count × cost per incident), customer churn attributable, SLA penalties, support escalations.

3. Cost of what we miss — deals lost where this capability was needed, customers we can't serve, capabilities competitors have that we don't.

4. Compounding analysis — what gets worse over 12, 24, 36 months. Debt and capability gaps grow non-linearly. Workaround cost grows with usage.

5. Risk costs — risks that increase if the investment isn't made: security exposure, compliance gap widening, reliability degradation. Probability × impact.

6. 3-year status-quo total — running cost of doing nothing. The number to compare to the investment ask.

Keep total under 700 words. Status quo is the comparison; make it expensive (because it usually is).

prompts.name: Return quantifier
prompts.use_case: Quantify the returns of an engineering investment when direct revenue impact isn't obvious.
inputs: Indirect impact paths (productivity, quality, capability, retention)
Anchors for translation (loaded engineering cost, customer LTV, etc.)
output_format: 'Productivity returns' — engineer-hours saved × loaded cost
'Quality returns' — incidents avoided × cost per incident, defect rate × cost per defect
'Revenue-enabled returns' — deals enabled, segments unlocked, customers retained
'Cost-avoidance returns' — vendor retired, headcount avoided, infrastructure savings
'Capability returns' — strategic optionality enabled (qualitative)
'Confidence per row' — high / medium / low
'3-year return total' — quantified portion
review_checklist: Productivity returns use realistic time-savings, not aspirational
Confidence levels are honest (most engineering returns are medium-low confidence)
Revenue-enabled is specific where possible (named segments, named customers)
Total presents what we can quantify; clearly distinguished from qualitative narrative
prompts.prompt:
You are quantifying the returns of an engineering investment. When direct revenue isn't obvious, build the indirect chain.

Context
- Investment proposal: {proposal}
- Indirect impact paths: {impact_paths}
- Translation anchors: {anchors}

Produce seven sections:

1. Productivity returns — engineer-hours saved per week × fully-loaded hourly cost × 50 weeks. Show calculation. Be honest about adoption ramp (productivity gains often take 6–12 months to fully realize).

2. Quality returns — incidents avoided × cost per incident (incident response cost + customer impact), defect rate × cost per defect.

3. Revenue-enabled returns — deals enabled (specific where possible: named segments, named customers, contract sizes), customer retention impact.

4. Cost-avoidance returns — vendors retired (specific contracts), headcount avoided (don't need to hire X because of capability), infrastructure savings.

5. Capability returns — strategic optionality enabled (qualitative; named in next prompt).

6. Confidence per row — high / medium / low. Most engineering returns are medium-low. Honest.

7. 3-year return total — quantified portion. Distinguish clearly from qualitative narrative; don't conflate.

Keep total under 600 words. Realistic quantification; honest confidence.

prompts.name: Qualitative-return narrator
prompts.use_case: Narrate the returns that resist quantification: capability, optionality, defensibility, talent, brand.
inputs: Strategic context (where we are, where we're going)
output_format: 'Strategic optionality' — what new options this enables (markets, partnerships, pivots)
'Capability building' — organizational capability that compounds
'Talent / morale' — ability to attract or retain key talent
'Brand / customer trust' — reputation effects
'Defensibility' — competitive moat created or maintained
'How to weight qualitative vs quantitative' — framing for the audience
review_checklist: Optionality is specific (named markets, partnerships)
Capability building is concrete (specific skills, specific systems)
Brand/trust connects to specific customer reactions or industry positioning
Audience framing is honest about uncertainty
prompts.prompt:
You are narrating the qualitative returns of an engineering investment. These resist quantification but are real.

Context
- Investment proposal: {proposal}
- Strategic context: {strategic_context}
- Audience: {audience}

Produce six sections:

1. Strategic optionality — what new options this investment enables. Specific: 'preserves the option to enter the [named] market in 2027'; 'enables partnership with [type of company] that requires SOC 2 we currently lack'.

2. Capability building — organizational capability that compounds. 'Our engineers will have built the muscle for X, which compounds because Y future projects require it.'

3. Talent / morale — ability to attract or retain key talent. 'Senior engineers want to work on [type of system]; without this investment, we lose at the top of the funnel.'

4. Brand / customer trust — reputation effects. 'Customers in [segment] explicitly cite [reliability / capability] as their concern with us; this investment addresses it.'

5. Defensibility — competitive moat created or maintained. 'Our competitors are X months ahead on this; without investment, gap widens to a structural disadvantage.'

6. How to weight qualitative vs quantitative — framing for the audience. CFO will want to see quant first, qual second. Board may weight optionality and defensibility heavily. Be honest about the limitations of quantification for these returns.

Keep total under 600 words. Specific even when qualitative; named optionality.

prompts.name: Approval-objection responder
prompts.use_case: Anticipate the CFO/CEO/board objections to an investment ask and prepare honest responses.
inputs: Approver's known concerns and decision style
Recent context (budget pressure, prior similar asks, organizational mood)
output_format: 'Top 5 likely objections' — each with the objection and honest response
'The hardest objection' — the one you don't have a clean answer to
'What you'd accept as a counter' — partial wins (lower amount, phased, contingent)
'What you'd not accept' — where the investment fails if compromised
'When to escalate vs when to accept the no'
review_checklist: Top objections include real ones (budget pressure, why-now, why-this-amount, why-not-buy)
Hardest objection is real (where you genuinely don't have a strong answer)
Counter-acceptance is calibrated (don't fight every battle)
Won't-accept items are real (don't pretend everything is negotiable)

prompts.estimated_time: 45–90 minutes per prompt; full ROI case spans 1–3 weeks of analysis

prompts.title: Vendor Evaluation Pack
prompts.summary: Four prompts for vendor selection: evaluation matrix from RFI responses, reference-call synthesis, TCO narrative, and risk scanner (financial, security, lock-in).
prompts.why_it_matters: Vendor decisions are 3–5 year commitments often made in 3–5 weeks. Most evaluations over-weight feature parity and under-weight TCO, lock-in, and operational fit. These prompts force the deeper analysis.
when_to_use: Evaluating 3–5 vendors against an RFI
Synthesizing reference calls before final selection
Building the TCO narrative for the spend approver
Scanning a finalist for financial / security / lock-in risk before signing
not_for: Bypassing security/legal review for production-critical vendors
Selecting based on feature checklist alone
source_inspiration: Standard procurement frameworks
Will Larson on tooling and platform decisions
Internal CIO / CTO playbooks on vendor risk

inputs_required.name: Use case
inputs_required.description: What we're solving and why we're buying instead of building.

inputs_required.name: Vendor shortlist
inputs_required.description: 3–5 vendors evaluated, with their RFI responses.

inputs_required.name: Evaluation criteria
inputs_required.description: Pre-defined criteria with weights.

inputs_required.name: Constraints
inputs_required.description: Budget, timing, security/compliance requirements.

sections.name: Usage guidance
prompts: TCO over sticker price.
Reference calls catch what RFIs hide.
Lock-in is the unspoken cost.

prompts.name: Vendor evaluation matrix
prompts.use_case: Build the evaluation matrix from RFI responses, scored against pre-defined criteria with weights.
inputs: Use case and selection criteria with weights
output_format: 'Criteria recap' — pre-defined criteria and weights, before scoring
'Scoring matrix' — table: Criteria | Weight | Vendor A | B | C | D
'Per-vendor strengths and weaknesses' — synthesis paragraph each
'Score-to-recommendation gap analysis' — does the score match what would be the right call? If not, why?
'Outstanding questions per vendor' — what we still need from each before deciding
review_checklist: Criteria and weights are pre-defined; not adjusted to match preferred vendor
Scoring is based on RFI evidence, not impression
Score-to-recommendation gap section catches when the matrix and the gut differ (real signal)
Outstanding questions are specific
prompts.prompt:
You are building the vendor evaluation matrix. Pre-defined criteria; honest scoring.

Context
- Use case and criteria with weights: {use_case_criteria}
- RFI responses: {rfi_responses}
- Internal team notes: {internal_notes}

Produce five sections:

1. Criteria recap — pre-defined criteria and weights, BEFORE scoring. Don't adjust them to match the preferred vendor.

2. Scoring matrix — table: Criteria | Weight | Vendor A | Vendor B | Vendor C | Vendor D | Notes. Score based on RFI evidence; cite the evidence in notes.

3. Per-vendor strengths and weaknesses — synthesis paragraph for each vendor. What they're best at, what's the concern.

4. Score-to-recommendation gap analysis — does the score match what would be the right call? If your gut and the score differ, that's signal. Either fix the criteria/weights (if they were wrong) or trust the matrix (if your gut is wrong).

5. Outstanding questions per vendor — what we still need from each before deciding. Specific.

Keep total under 700 words. Honest scoring; explicit gaps.

prompts.name: Reference call synthesis
prompts.use_case: Synthesize reference calls (3+ per vendor) into actionable signal — what to weight, what to discount.
output_format: 'Per-vendor signal' — what the references confirmed, what they contradicted, what surprised us
'Quality of references' — vendor-provided are weaker signal; independent (we found via network) are stronger
'Cross-vendor patterns' — themes consistent across all vendor references (tells us about category)
'Red flags' — anything in the references that worries us
'What references didn't tell us' — what we'd still want to know but couldn't get
review_checklist: Vendor-provided references are weighted lower (they're cherry-picked)
Red flags are surfaced even from positive-overall references
Cross-vendor patterns separate vendor-specific from category-wide
Couldn't-get info is named so we know our blind spots
prompts.prompt:
You are synthesizing reference calls into actionable signal for vendor selection.

Context
- Reference call notes: {ref_notes}
- Questions asked: {questions}
- Reference quality: {ref_quality}

Produce five sections:

1. Per-vendor signal — what the references confirmed (matches RFI), what they contradicted (different from what vendor claimed), what surprised us (new info).

2. Quality of references — vendor-provided references are weaker (cherry-picked); independent references (found via your network) are stronger. Weight accordingly.

3. Cross-vendor patterns — themes consistent across all vendor references. Tells us about the category, not just the vendor (e.g., all of them have onboarding pain at month 2).

4. Red flags — anything that worries us, even from references that were positive overall. Specific quotes if you have them.

5. What references didn't tell us — questions we'd still want answered but couldn't get. Names our blind spots before signing.

Keep total under 600 words. References reveal what RFIs hide.

prompts.name: TCO narrative
prompts.use_case: Build the Total Cost of Ownership narrative — license + implementation + ongoing operations + opportunity cost.
inputs: Ongoing operations cost (admin time, training, monitoring)
output_format: 'Year 1 TCO' — implementation + license + operations
'Year 2–3 TCO' — recurring + scale-related growth
'TCO breakdown' — table by category with assumptions
'Hidden costs' — costs vendors don't quote (your eng time on integration, training)
'Opportunity cost' — what your team's not doing because they're operating this vendor
'TCO-vs-build narrative' — vs. building in-house, including ongoing engineering cost
review_checklist: Year 1 includes implementation honestly
Hidden costs are quantified (engineering time = engineering cost)
Opportunity cost is named (your team's other work)
Build comparison includes ongoing engineering cost, not just initial dev
prompts.prompt:
You are building the TCO narrative for a vendor decision. Sticker price is the smallest part.

Context
- Vendor pricing: {pricing}
- Implementation cost: {implementation_cost}
- Ongoing operations cost: {ops_cost}
- Time horizon: {horizon}

Produce six sections:

1. Year 1 TCO — implementation (eng + services + integration) + license + ongoing operations setup. Honest implementation cost, not vendor-quoted.

2. Year 2–3 TCO — recurring license + ongoing operations + scale-related cost growth.

3. TCO breakdown — table by category with assumptions.

4. Hidden costs — costs vendors don't quote: your engineering time on integration and ongoing maintenance, training cost, monitoring/observability of the vendor, escape cost if you switch later.

5. Opportunity cost — what your team is NOT doing because they're operating this vendor. Real even if not in the budget.

6. TCO-vs-build narrative — vs. building in-house. Include ongoing engineering cost of build (typically the dominant cost; initial dev is the smaller part). Honest comparison.

Keep total under 700 words. TCO is the honest cost; sticker price isn't.

prompts.name: Vendor risk scan
prompts.use_case: Scan a finalist vendor for financial / security / lock-in risk before signing.
inputs: Finalist vendor info (company size, funding, customer concentration, recent news)
Contract terms (lock-in clauses, data portability, termination)
output_format: 'Financial health' — funding stage, runway, dependence on a single segment, M&A risk
'Security posture' — certifications, recent incidents, data handling, breach disclosure history
'Lock-in risk' — data portability, contract auto-renewal, switching cost
'Operational risk' — SLA, support quality, escalation paths
'Compliance risk' — regulatory fit (GDPR, SOX, HIPAA as applicable)
'Mitigations' — what we'd negotiate to reduce each risk
'Walk-away triggers' — risks that would change the recommendation
review_checklist: Financial health includes funding-stage signal
Lock-in analysis includes data portability and switching cost
Mitigations are concrete (what to negotiate, what to add to contract)
Walk-away triggers are real (don't pretend everything is acceptable)
prompts.prompt:
You are scanning a finalist vendor for risk before signing. Multi-year commitments deserve scrutiny.

Context
- Finalist vendor info: {vendor_info}
- Security posture: {security}
- Contract terms: {contract_terms}

Produce seven sections:

1. Financial health — funding stage, runway, customer concentration, M&A risk. Early-stage vendors carry continuity risk; late-stage vendors carry pricing-power risk.

2. Security posture — SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / SOC 3, recent incidents, data handling practices, breach disclosure history.

3. Lock-in risk — data portability (can we get our data out?), contract auto-renewal (how much notice to terminate?), switching cost (eng time + dual-running cost).

4. Operational risk — SLA terms (and historical achievement), support quality, escalation paths, on-call coverage.

5. Compliance risk — regulatory fit for our context (GDPR, SOX, HIPAA, industry-specific).

6. Mitigations — what we'd negotiate to reduce each risk. Concrete contract terms.

7. Walk-away triggers — risks that, if confirmed, change the recommendation. Pre-commit so we don't rationalize past them in the moment.

Keep total under 700 words. Multi-year commitments warrant the scrutiny.
